=== n8n Is Not Enough Anymore ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1t8Fs6cndB8

I've built hundreds of NAND workflows and I've taught thousands of people how to use it and for the first time I'm telling people to stop mostly and that's not because NAND is bad. It's definitely not. But something has changed over the last 6 months which has altered the calculus about when you should and should not use NADN to solve your problems. And if you're building AI systems for yourself or for a client, it is imperative that you understand where that line is because it's not in the same place it used to be. Up until about six months ago, if you wanted to create a complex AI automation or agent, the answer was pretty clear as for what tool you should use, especially if you were non-technical, and that was NADN because the alternatives at the time was either writing real code or using the earlier versions of these agent coding agents like claude code or cursor. And now those agentic coding agents were very strong. But the truth was to get the most out of them you did need to understand how to write code. You had to get your hands dirty very often. But now that's completely changed because tools like claw code, codeex, and anti-gravity have gotten so good and so userfriendly that you don't need to know how to code to get the most out of these. And if you are using those particular tools, the ceiling for what you can do with them has actually passed NADN. And because of that dynamic, NAND's limitations have become a lot harder to ignore. So in this video, we're going to dive deep into those limitations. So you can come out of here with a better sense of when NAND makes sense and when it doesn't because this is not by any means an NAN hit piece. Naden is simply a tool, but we need to be educated about when Naden is the best tool for the job and when we should start using coding agents like cloud code. So let's kick this off by talking about the three primary areas that NAD struggles when we compare it to agentic coding agents like cloud code. And remember it's not just cloud code. You could insert anti-gravity, codeex, open code, any of those, right? But I'll continue to reference cloud code throughout this video. Now those three areas where naden struggles are scalability, speed to deployment on complex projects, and commercial licensing. Now let's start with scaling. And there's two parts to scaling. First part is scaling of the project itself. So imagine I have some sort of project that's inside of NAND and it's now 50 or 60 nodes. Might sound extreme, but there are use cases where we need this. And the issue with NAN is once we get to those wild levels of code nodes and just nodes in general, it starts to become rather brittle. And as it becomes more brittle and larger in general, chances are we're also tossing in a lot more code nodes in our workflow itself, which then begs the question, why are we even in NN in the first place? But as you take your workflow and add more deeply nested logic, more loops, more error handling, you begin to see some of the issues with the NN infrastructure because the equivalent of those 50 60 node solutions inside of NAND could very easily just be a few hundred lines of code inside of something like cloud code which is much easier to test and scale. Which brings us to the second point of scaling and that's throughput and thinking of how many users might be using this thing all at once. If we're trying to run hundreds of things in parallel, deal with thousands of API calls every hour and deal with very large data sets, chances are again NAN is going to struggle here. And the solutions for those scenarios inside of NAND eventually lead you towards some hacky type things, right? Where you're kind of going outside of the box with NN to make it work. And when you get to the place where you're trying to do hacky solutions to actually solve your problem with any again you need to ask yourself why am I doing this and not using a tool like cloud code and using real code to solve the problem in the manner is meant to be solved because when you're handling these large complex projects inevitably you begin to hit bottlenecks inside of naden and there's easy solutions inside of a normal coding ecosystem to solve that. Now, the second issue with NAND is its speed to deployment when it comes to complex projects. Now, this might surprise some of you because speed to deployment is kind of NAND's thing, but it's really only Naden's thing when it's with simple or medium difficulty projects. When we're dealing with complex issues, the fact is it is infinitely faster for me to hop inside of NAND or Codeex and prompt my way to the solution versus going inside of NADN and trying to tie a bunch of nodes together. And that also includes if I'm on any cloud version, which isn't available on the self-host, and that's their workflow builder. Furthermore, the output I get with real code is going to be easier to iterate upon, easier to test, and easier to add things on to it later down the line. Now, the third area where NAND struggles is commercial licensing. And this doesn't really get talked about enough, and I think it's because NAD's fair use policy is very confusing. NAD is not open source, and I've seen it referenced as open source by a lot of creators, and that's simply not true. It uses a fair use policy. Now, what does that mean? Well, that means if I build something that's awesome inside of any and I want to sell it or use it as a SAS project, well, I can, but only in certain instances. And those instances are if my application I built an NN does not require the user to give me credentials. So, imagine I built a personal assistant. Imagine I built my version of of OpenClaw inside of Nen. Well, that might require you to give me your email credentials or your Gmail credentials or whatever credentials. can't do that inside of NAN, right? And there's many many scenarios you can imagine that would require the customer to give their credentials for whatever reason to make the app work. You can't do that in NAND. And what that means is you're very limited in what you can create inside of NAN and then turn around and make a SAS project. So in terms of using NADN to build something to make money, again this falls very very short. Again, compare that to something like cloud code. I build it. It's my code. I can sell it however I want. I can package it however I want. I can take things that are open source. Not the case with NAND. And that's a huge issue if you're someone who's in this to build to make money. So those are the three areas where NAND struggles and they're areas that are becoming more and more apparent in today's day and age. Now like I said in the beginning, this is not an any hit piece. Any is a tool and it's the best tool for the job in many instances. But what is its lane? When does it make sense to use? Let's talk about that now because I think there's three specific places where NAND should still be your go-to because there's still three areas where Naden wins. Number one is its speed to deployment with simple or medium difficulty projects. Number two is its accessibility for non-technical teams and number three is its entire integration ecosystem. So let's talk about those. So let's start with number one which is its speed to deployment with simpler medium-sized projects. N is still king here. If I want to put together a very quick MVP with a relatively linear idea that I know how I want it to start from beginning to end, I can do that in Nen in 5 10 minutes and I can do it in a visual manner which is very easy to both explain to a non-technical team but also troubleshoot and iterate upon versus if I try to do something like that in clawed code, it involves a lot of additional overhead that may just not make sense at these early stages. And that speed to deployment on these lower level projects, a lot of this glue work, that sort of internal tools behind the scenes isn't something you can dismiss out of hand. And it is where NAD is 100% a winner. And piggybacking off of that is point number two, which is accessibility to non-technical teams. And this is not something that can be dismissed. A large part of NAND's meteoric rise was its accessibility. It was the ability to allow people who weren't technical to actually get their hands dirty and start doing things that were completely outside their wheelhouse a few years ago, but were actually realistic for them to build because it was visual, because it was relatively simple. So, if you're dealing with a client or a team that is full of people who quote unquote aren't technical, yet they need to get hands-on on this project, may need to edit it, may need to actually troubleshoot it, well, NN is brilliant for that. Versus the alternative, which is like, hey, I built this Python app for you. Here's the GitHub repo. Go ahead and edit it as you need. Well, that's a bridge too far, frankly, for honestly 99% of the population. So if you're building things for teams of non-developers who do need to get hands-on, frankly, NADN totally shines in that department. And lastly, the integration system of NADN is pretty unmatched. There's what 4 500 built-in integrations inside of NADN that is essentially abstract away the entire authentication process, which frankly can be a pain in the butt even if you're using something like cloud code. And that integration ecosystem and that abstraction layer is again huge for some of this internal background glue work that essentially keeps a lot of businesses running with low-level automations. So let's kind of recap where we are in 2026 with all these tools because the gap between NAND and these coding agents like claude code used to be a crack, but now it's a canyon and it's a canyon that's getting larger and larger. But that doesn't mean NAND is dying. It just means nen has a very specific lane now. And while again 6 months to 12 months ago the highway was kind of dominated by nen well now maybe it just has a couple lanes on the right hand side and that's okay because again it's a tool. You're an AI developer. You should be somewhat tool agnostic. We just want to solve problems and use the correct tool for the job. This isn't a hit on nan. This isn't me trying to hype up cloud code. It's just the reality. And if you want to become a more mature AI developer, it is imperative you understand which lane you're in for which particular job. Which means the true power is in knowing how to do both. We do not want to be a one-trick pony. But I also think it's important to have these conversations, especially in the AI space where these things are just changing so so rapidly. So that's where I'm going to leave you for today. As always, check out the links in the description and in the comments if you're looking for more resources about both Claude Code and NNN because I think they still have a ton of value no matter where you are in your AI journey. Tons of resources in the free school. I also have Chase AI Plus which is if you're a little more serious about this and you're trying to figure out, hey, how can I actually use these skills to make money? So, make sure to check that out as well. And as always, I'll see you

=== i converted all my n8n agents to real code and it was stupid easy ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dSp4jL8R2o0

NADN has been one of the most powerful noode tools on the market for some time now. But today, as platforms like Claude Code, Codeex, and anti-gravity become both more powerful and more userfriendly, one of the skills you need to master is taking your NADN automations, agents, and workflows and being able to convert them into proper applications when called for. Now this is something we have touched on in previous videos where we have taken any automations and used cloud code to essentially build a wrapper around them. We've given them a front end, a backend, databases, the works. But at its core, it was still an NAN JSON file essentially powering all the logic. But in today's video, we're taking it a step further. We aren't creating a wrapper this time because instead we're going to be taking an NAND JSON file and turning it into a proper Python app. And for today's demo, we are going to be converting one of my favorite NAND workflows. It's a Rag AI agent powered by LightRag. But this video isn't about any one-off project. What this video is really about is giving you a framework to convert any NAND automation you want while also having a nuance discussion about when this sort of conversion when this transformation from NAN to real code actually makes sense and when it doesn't and what the pros and cons really are. So with that let's get to it. So let's take a look at the AI agent we are going to be converting today. This is a RAG AI agent. Rag stands for retrieval augmented generation and essentially that means our AI agent is hooked up to a vector database in this case a graph rag system using light rag which allows it to take on a bunch of documentation and then answer questions about it right that's essentially all that means and there's three parts to this AI agent you have the AI agent system right here which allows you to actually query and ask questions second you have a data pipeline so we need some way to actually ingest docu douments into the system. Right? If I want to ask this AI agent questions about meeting notes, well, how do I get the meeting notes into the vector database? That's what this does. And then third, we need some sort of data cleanup system, right? So imagine I uploaded meeting notes into the database. Well, what happens if I delete it from the database? Maybe I put in the wrong thing. Does that get deleted in the vector database? Right? You need some sort of data syncing system. So all of that exists here inside of N&NN and it works well, but it's three disperate systems and it's in one JSON file and we essentially want to push it into real code using cloud code for three reasons. And these three reasons don't just apply to this light rag AI agent in a vacuum. This sort of applies in general as well. And the three big reasons and these aren't exhaustive is performance, cost, and control. First of all, let's talk about performance. Right now, this rag agent lives inside of NAND. That's either going to be on the NAND cloud or if you're self-hosted on whatever cloud you host NADN on. But the light rag system itself, due to the way NAN is set up, cannot live inside of NAND, it's going to have to be on its own server somewhere else. And that comes at a performance cost. Every time I talk to this AI agent here inside of NADN, right? It now has to take the trip over to the graph rag server. Figure out your answer and bring it back. That costs us time. That also moving on to point two cost us money, right? I'm now dealing with servers for NN. I'm now dealing with servers for light rag. I'm now now dealing with the cost associated with running both of those and having both talk to one another. Now compare that to when we can have it operate as an actual Python application. Light rag itself is actually a Python library which means I can create all of this everything we see here in NAN using Python code and have the graph rag interface database that whole system just be a part of that code essentially meaning all of it is in one place right that reduces my cost that increases performance everything is in a nice neat little box and before you say hey Chase couldn't we just do like a code node here or something and do Python and add libraries into all this stuff. Well, then the question becomes, why are we actually inside of NAND if we're just scaffolding a bunch of code notes together? And then that brings us into point three, which is control. And this is rather obvious. When I'm working inside of NAND, I am constrained by the platform itself, both in terms of its tools and sort of legality issues, right? Think about the naden fair use policy and where that can constrain you. Especially if you're trying to create something like a SAS application where you want to get paid for it versus if I create this as a Python application by myself with my code, I own it all, right? I don't even have to think about that. So I also have the entire breadth and width of every coding tool imaginable at my disposal, right? And I h and I can access those things in a less hacky way than creating something inside of any where it's a bunch of code nodes slapped together. Now, this is not to say NIDN does not have its place. And at the end of this video, we're going to dive into that and I'm really going to talk about, hey, here's where NAD does make sense because it does still play a part in your 2026 AI stack. But understand, there are some major advantages to moving away from NADN into whatever you want to call it, real code or whatever using something like log code. So, now that you understand what we're converting and why we're doing it, now let's go through the how. And the great part about this is it's actually pretty easy. We are going to hop into Claude Code. We are going to create a new project folder. And I'm just going to dump the JSON file that I downloaded from NADN into the project folder. I'm then going to go into plan mode. And alls I'm going to do is I'm going to say, hey, I put in this NADN automation into the project folder. I want to convert it into a real application. I want to get outside of the JSON structure. What do we need to do for this? That's pretty much it. That's pretty much all you have to do for almost everything. No matter what automation agent workflow you're doing, that's what you got to do. Now, if you have a well-created JSON file like mine, and maybe you have some stuff written over here about how it works, this will go very, very smoothly. Right? If what you're trying to convert inside of NAND already works, well, guess what? The JSON file is telling Claude Code what it's supposed to do. It's just rewriting that in essentially a different way using Python or whatever it happens to use. Now, obviously, there's 10 billion different iterations of Naden workflows. All I wrote here was I want to convert this NAD JSON rag agent into a proper app. Take a look at the file. It's in the folder and tell me what you think. it's going to go to work. And again, we're set up so well in this scenario versus like creating some sort of application from thin air using cloud code. We've already done all the hard work inside of NAND, right? You really have. Now, as this is spinning up, let's talk about though some of the roadblocks that you will probably face and they're going to be related to authentication because inside of NAND, you've probably already gone through the painstaking authentication process inside of things like Google, for example. OpenClaw is the fastest growing open-source AI project of all time. And somehow, even with all that hype behind it, there is not a single good guide that shows you how to properly set up this thing in the cloud and connect the one tool you need to actually turn it into a proper AI assistant, and that's your Google account. Connecting OpenClaw to your Google Workspace is a giant pain in the butt, which is why all these AI influencers skip this step entirely when they show you how to set up OpenClaw. So, let's first talk about the hosting options very quickly. Three big options for where OpenClaw can live. Number one, and this is what you see all over the place, is a Mac Mini. This is expensive, right? This is going to run you $400 to $600, but you can connect it to your Mac and it's going to be up and running all the time. Your second option is to host it somewhere on the cloud. So you're paying some third party say hosting your $7 to $10 a month. Again, very cheap to essentially give you space on one of their servers, which is where OpenCloud will live. Third option is to host it directly on your computer. So it's not in the cloud, it's also not on your Mac Mini, it's right on your machine. Now, the downside with having it right on your machine is if you want OpenClaw running all the time, well then your machine needs to be running all the time, which for most people that's just not the case. Whether you're on Linux or Windows or a Mac, everyone can do this, right? So, I think this is going to be the easiest option for most people. This doesn't require you to shell out money for a mini. also doesn't require you to necessarily be on a Mac and it's probably the most safe option as well. And really the great value in OpenClaw I think is what you just saw there how easy it is to connect it to different things. Right? All I did was I spoke to it, right? Compare that with like an automation where we would create it with a personal assistant. I'd have to go in there and I'd have to create the module to have to set up the credentials. Here with OpenClaw, a lot of that's kind of abstracted away. I just need to speak to it. So, OpenClaw is now installed on the VPS. We can talk to it through Telegram. Now comes the fun part, actually hooking it up to Google. Now, this is sort of a multi-step process to do this. First, we're going to head to Google Workspace. We're going to give our agent its own email. We're going to connect it to the appropriate APIs. Then, we're going to come back to our VPS, hop into the terminal. We're going to install the Gogg CLI. Go. This is the tool that allows OpenClaw and Google to actually communicate because unfortunately it is it isn't as easy as it was with Telegram where we can just talk to OpenClaw and have it do itself. After we do that, we're going to authenticate all of our accounts and then we'll test it out and see if it's actually working as it should. So, a lot of moving parts, but we're going to go through it step by step so you can follow along. So, the first thing we're going to do is we're going to head to Gmail and we're going to create a new email for our agent. Now, this is a level of separation that's meant to introduce some safety, right? If I want Open Claw to send emails, I don't want it to do it with my email address, right? I don't necessarily want my name and everything in my account just at its fingertips. Also, by having its own email and having its own accounts, it can still interact with us, right? It can still take a look at our calendar. I can add it to the calendar. I can still give it access to my Google Drive. I can specify specific folders, right? This is the idea of least privilege, right? I don't want to give it everything because it might want to use it. We're going to start small. Now, the one caveat to this is, hey, what if I do want it to look at my inbox? Well, that can be kind of complicated. And you can indeed give it access to your account if that's what you want to do. Just go into these decisions like that with your eyes wide open and understanding what's at risk because we don't have to risk everything if we don't want to. So, go ahead, create the account and we'll move on to the next step, which is the workspace. What you need to make sure is that you're on your agents email account. So, if you come up here to the top right, make sure that the account it's associated with is the bots account. It's not yours. If you need to switch it, just click on the name and switch accounts. Next, I want you to create a new project. So, click my first project up here. Do new project. Call it whatever you want. When I spin up an agent team, it's not only going to have these like sub aents that are doing those things, it's also going to create a team lead, right? We now essentially have a middle manager in the loop. And so these sub aents, right, these team members will talk to and report to this team lead who coordinates everything and make sure everything makes sense in the greater hole. But it goes a little bit deeper than that because these sub aents can actually talk to one another. So database can talk to backend, backend can talk to UI, UI can talk to database, so on and so forth. And what that means is we can actually work on much more complex projects and expect better outcomes versus the typical sub aent system which is much more attuned for like one-off tasks, right? Things that can be done in a vacuum, right? I just need to send this guy off over here to the ether to do this one task and do it well and come back to me. The era of just building NADN workflows is over. You know that custom NAD automation you created for a client and sold for 23 $4,000? But it's not just about charging more. When we step outside of the Naden canvas and begin to enter the world of tools like Clawude Code, we can add way more functionality to these automations and agents we've built inside of NAND. And by doing so, you begin to build custom software and no longer are stuck being a one-trick pony tying nodes together. Now, don't get me wrong, I still love NAN, but Naden is simply a tool and it's a tool with certain strengths and certain weaknesses. But luckily for us, the weaknesses of NADN are actually strengths for aentic coders like Claude code. So if we hope to become more mature AI developers, it is important that we understand how to use these two tools together in order to create better products. So for our example today, we have this particular NAND automation and I'll explain quickly how it works. So, the general idea is that it's going to scrape the web, particularly social media, to see what's trending in our niche in the last 24 hours, and then generate ideas that can be used to create LinkedIn posts. So, it scrapes YouTube, gets all the data, it scrapes X, looking for specific keywords that also fit certain like thresholds, and it has a catch-all with perplexity. It sends all this data to AI which then synthesizes it all and says, "Hey, here's five potential ideas for LinkedIn posts based on this trending stuff." Easy enough. Now, this is actually based on some client work I did. It was actually for Twitter instead, but in this case, we're using LinkedIn. And the idea was they wanted something similar, but I couldn't just hand them this workflow, right? Because they wanted to have a little more hands-on interaction with it, right? They didn't want it to just generate posts for them. They wanted to see the post it would generate, choose the one they liked, and then also edit it in some sort of interface, right? That's kind of difficult to do inside of NAN. Yet, that's exactly what I'm going to show you how to do here because what do you see here? Same exact thing. Over the last 24 hours, it searched trending AI topics. It gave us these five, right? Okay. Here's five ideas. Do I want it to generate an article? If I want to generate an article, I just click on it. I then go to view and you can see it generated an article about clawed code and reotion. Okay, you've probably seen some videos about that lately. And so it comes up with this article, but that's not enough, right? I want to be able to edit this thing. So it has some presets, right? Make it more professional, more casual, more authorative. You know, actually, I want it shorter. I want it longer, stronger hook, whatever. I can even give it specific edits with this AI assistant. And it has a version history. So, we've added a ton of functionality and ease of use for someone who wants that base and an automation but packaged a little differently. And you can also imagine, hey, what this is worth to someone and what this is worth to someone are two different things cuz truthfully, again, kind of putting on on our AI agency hat, nobody cares about this, right? No clients care about what's going on in the background. So that's what I'm going to show you how to do and the first thing we need to talk about is what sort of questions and what sort of things do you need to be thinking about as we transition from workflow to AI system. So you have your edit in workflow and we want to use cloud code to build this wrapper around it to turn it into a true product. Well, there's a few things we have to ask and we're going to cover five of them right now. And let's first talk about why it's important to even have these conversations at all. Because the truth is, if you have a simple automation and you're just putting a simple front end on it, cloud code can do it just fine. Even if you were blind, deaf, and dumb, right? It's intelligent enough to figure that out. However, in order for us to not become a caricature of a Vive coder, it's important we understand how this process works. You don't need to know the code. You don't ever need to write a single line of code and understand how Python syntax works or TypeScript works. We really don't, right? That's just the truth of the matter these days. But you do need to understand how these building blocks all come together to create it because as good as cloud code is, it isn't perfect. And more importantly, as you move on to more complicated projects with more complicated automations making up the foundation of the functionality, well, if you have no clue how anything is working, what's the point of you even being there? And it's also just not going to work, right? Because as good as cloud code is, it's not perfect. and you do still need to guide it in certain directions, but if you don't even know, you know, how to go from A to B, let alone what A and B even are, you got no shot when it comes down to it, and you're just wasting money. So, these conversations are important. And the first thing we need to think about as we make this transition from any automation to AI system is sort of obvious, what are the functionality changes, right? what's going to change as we move from, you know, this JSON file over here to what we want to do, you know, in an interface. So, the original incantation of this incarnation of this was it just created the LinkedIn post for you, right? It just sent it to your email. That's not good enough. Now, what are the changes we wanted? Well, we don't just want the post right away. We want five topics to choose from. Okay, that's going to be different. So, the data that comes out there needs to be different. Well, what's going to happen when they see the five topics? Well, I want to be able to choose wrong one and then I want the page to come up with the post. All right, is that good enough? No. I also want to be able to have the presets and I want to be able to edit it and I want version changes. Okay, that's sort of the the functionality difference. That's sort of the flow that the user is going to go through for our particular thing. Those are things you need to know. Okay, that being said, cloud code will help walk you through that. Now, the second thing you need to be thinking about is data. Now, this is really important and sometimes not super obvious. First of all, from the NN side, how is data getting into this automation and how is it going out and what sort of starts that process? This used to originally be a scheduling trigger, right? It would just run every single morning. Well, the user actually wanted it to be on demand. So, that changes how data is coming, right? So, how's that process going to work? What data is and then what data is coming on the backside? Right? Remember I said before it used to be the full-blown LinkedIn post. Now that data just needs to be the five topics. Also, what form is that data coming in, right? Is it going to be a markdown down file? Is it going to be JSON? Like what does the data look like? Furthermore, kind of easier to see when you see the finished product. But hey, remember how we said we wanted like version history of what we're doing right down here. Well, if I want version history, where does that data where where do those versions live? Right? Do I want to be able to save these posts? Do I want some sort of database to hold all this information? What will that database look like? Again, these are things you need to be thinking about. Then we're going to want to sort of talk about UI and UX, user interface and user experience. Now, the nice thing about the UIUX conversation is it is largely a function of a the functionality changes and b the data, right? The user flow, how we think this person will actually interact with our product should dictate where buttons are, what it looks like, what sort of the pages, what what is the user journey, right? Now, in terms of purely aesthetics, right, what do I want my web page to look like? In this case, I said, hey, just use like an anthropic clawed aesthetic. So let's take some time to talk about common pitfalls that I think anyone new to the space will encounter, no matter what it is they're actually creating here. And the first one is testing and sort of the local host dynamic. So I think this is easiest to explain visually. So local testing, what does that mean? Why do we do that? So we always want to test locally. We don't want to test in production. So when we are first building our app, it's going to be done on our computer. We're not going to take our half-finish app, give it a domain, put it on the web, and potentially expose, you know, error, bugridden code that people, potentially bad actors, can poke at. Furthermore, down the line, when our app is fleshed out and I want to make changes to it, well, I don't want to do that in production. and I don't want to take the entire app offline every time I'm testing something. So, we need a safe place where we can test. That's where the local development server comes in. This just means everything's essentially running on our computer. So, when Cloud Code tells you, hey, I spun up a dev server and you can check out your app on localhost 3000. What is it saying? Well, it's saying, hey, I built your app. It's on a computer. You can find it here at this address. That will take you to a browser. it's not on the internet yet we can still interact with things on the internet which is great again for testing. So that being said there are some like nuances with how we are able to take this thing on our computer and interact with and have things interact with us that are on the web. So, two situations. Situation number one, I have an NAND workflow that runs on a timer, and I want it to send me every single morning at 8 a.m. a content report about what's trending on social media to my web app that's on my local host. Okay, it starts here and it sends it here. Scenario number two, I want to add some sort of chatbot functionality to my app, right? I want to connect it to Open AI. So, I want to be able to send a request to OpenAI and then have it send me information back, right? My prompt, its response. Which one of these will actually work inside of a local host off the bat? The answer is number two. This will work and this one will not work. That's a problem because in many scenarios, we need both of these to work. We need to test both these things. So, first, why is it this way? Well, in this scenario, when I'm talking with OpenAI, I am sending information there first. It's like me going to OpenAI and having a letter that says, "Hey, I need you to do X, Y, and Z." And on that letter is my address. So, it knows where to go. Versus this scenario, I never sent it anything. This is essentially just a web hook out on the web that's being told to send information somewhere. Yet, if on my local host, it's not going to be able to find it. So, how do we solve this problem in a local testing scenario? Well, what we need is we need some sort of like tunneling system or IP forwarding system. So, I almost need like a temporary IP address that actually does live on the internet that points to my local host. This is where Enro comes in. Enro will supply you that temporary IP. So in scenarios where like our NADN automation that sends something every morning on its schedule, it instead sends it to this IP which then sends it to our local host. The great thing about this is Enro is basically free for all intents and purposes and it takes about 60 seconds to set up and cloud code knows exactly how to do that. However, this whole scenario is not obvious to someone who has never worked in this environment before and is not technical, which is why I took the time to show you this. So, you do need to know how this scenario works cuz I will promise you Cloud Code will screw this up. Cloud Code is smart, but for whatever reason, I don't know why it's always, at least for me, messing up when and when not to use Enro. Now, as for how to use it inside Cloud Code, just ask Cloud Code, walk me through setting up Enro. The first time takes 60 seconds and every time beyond that, it's literally as easy as like typing in nrok. So easy fix. Again, you just need to understand when and where this would come into play. And the other thing you have to keep in mind is once we do have our app in production and we've been testing with this IP like, hey, we told NADN, hey, always send it to this IP. Well, when we have a real domain and we're ready to do this for real, we would tell it, hey, use the real domain now. So, just understand there are some changes at the end you'd have to make. So, you're going to head to versell.com. When you create the account, make sure you link your GitHub account. It'll make it super easy. From there, you'll go onto this page, just your projects overview, and then you'll just hit add new project. It's then going to pull up a list of all the projects inside of your GitHub repository. And that message then goes to a gateway. Now, the gateway, imagine, is like this switchboard. And from here, it's going to pass it off to an actual AI brain, say Claude or OpenAI or Gemini. And then from there, it's going to use a number of tools and skills to get the job done. So, it hits this gateway and then it goes to Opus 4.5. So, now Opus 4.5 is driving the car. And Opus 4.5 has a number of tools and skills at its disposal. Off the bat, Claudebot has 52 tools at its disposal, and those tools have associated skills. Now, one of those tools is the Gmail tool. So, it has the ability to actually say, "Hey, Google, I have access to my user's Gmail account. I want to take a look at his Gmail and I want to grab all of his emails over the last eight hours. So then Gmail sends that information back to Opus 4.5 which sends the information back to the gateway where Claudebot lives. This information gets saved on the gateway and we'll go into that more detail in a second. At this point it's also taken a look at the emails over here in the AI brain and given it a summary of like hey here's everything that happened in the last 8 hours that goes back to Telegram which goes back to you. And so what happened was you opened Telegram, you told Cloudbot what's been going on in my inbox over the last 8 hours. It went through this loop. It checked your email, created a summary, and sent it back to you. Kind of makes sense. Just as if you had a real human assistant, said, "Hey, go check my email and tell me what's been going on." Works the same way. Now, let's dive into this a little bit more because nobody would be going crazy over an AI bot that can just check your email. Like I said, it has 52 tools and associated skills essentially preloaded into its program. Now, what that means is obviously it can do things like check your email, check your calendar, but it also has the ability to interact with your operating system. It can run code, it can run terminal commands, it can run cloud code for you. It can actually interact with your machine. On top of that, it has other really cool tools, especially if you're on Mac. Things like Peekaboo. It can open up its own web browser. It can take screenshots for the browser to see what's going on. It can fill out forms. It can book a flight for you. It can order food for you. So, there's a lot of really interesting use cases for this thing that do give it a leg up versus say an NAND personal assistant, especially in terms of this like operating system interface. What is this all about? because you've probably seen people saying Claudebot has infinite memory and it can know everything about you. So the gateway, this is where Claudebot actually lives, okay? And it can live in a number of places. So for many people, it is right here. It's the Mac Mini. Now, why would you get a Mac Mini to run this? Well, Cloudbot is only running if its gateway is running and the Mac Mini doesn't turn off. So if I had it on my computer and I turn off my computer and I try to then talk to Cloudbot, well, it's not going to work, right? already got turned off. Versus the Mac Mini, I can have it run essentially forever. The other option, right, besides the mini and your computer is just hosting it in the cloud. Using something like that, and it doesn't require a lot of power either. Now, in terms of infinite memory, it's not really infinite, but it might as well be. It's just saving all of your conversations in a database, and that database is a SQL light database. So every single conversation you have gets put inside the database and it's able to reference those past conversations essentially through a rag system. It's 70% semantic, 30% keyword search. So if I tell it something like I only go to the gym on Mondays, right? You're super lazy, but you only go on Mondays. And you then ask Claudebot, hey, it's Monday. What should I be doing today? It will then be able to look through the database, see that, oh yeah, this lazy dude only goes to the gym on Mondays and it's Monday. Hey buddy, it's time to go to the gym. Right? That's essentially how it works. And it still has context window things, right? Like it can if you're using the AI brain of say Opus 4.5, I can still only take, you know, 200,000 tokens. But this allows it to essentially on demand bring up those past conversations and insert it into the context window as needed. And speaking of the AI brains, you have multiple options, right? === Stop Using Ralph Loops (Use This Instead) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_-666PtaweU

You need to stop using Ralph loops. The Ralph loop hype is everywhere right now, and that's not without good reason. The actual Ralph loop fundamentals are actually really solid. Things like context window management, atomic tasks, and persistence until completion are all things we should be trying to incorporate into our cloud code workflows. But there are a lot of things people aren't telling you about Ralph loops. >> I'm in danger. First of all, most people aren't even using the correct type of Ralph loops. That Ralph Wiggum plugin you have in Claude Code, yeah, that's not a real Ralph loop. And I actually did a whole video on that topic. Secondly, as the creator of the Ralph loop stated himself, Ralph loops are simply a technique. At its most base form, it's just a bash loop and it's one piece of a larger puzzle when you are trying to create projects. And importantly, that piece, the loops, is entirely dependent on everything that comes before it. How good is your product requirements document? How tight did you define your features? How do you know what done actually looks like? If the answers to those questions aren't exact and correct, then you're just going to get stuck in a garbage in garbage out scenario, no matter how many times your loop runs. The Ralph Loop is an extremely powerful weapon, but most of us don't need a weapon. We need the entire armory, and that's where GSD comes in. While Ralph Loops assume you show up to the session with your entire blueprint ready to go, GSD actually helps you build it. GSD takes your half-baked idea, asks you deep questions about it, does research on your behalf, fleshes out a full-blown PRD, turns that PRD into atomic tasks for the AI to execute, and then completes the project from end to end. And while it's executing the code, it's using many of the same fundamentals that makes the Ralph loops as strong as they are. Namely, context window management using sub aents and the idea of giving AI as small and discreet of tasks as possible. so we always get the best output. So in this video, we're going to dive into GSD. I'm going to show you how to set it up. I'm going to show you what this framework actually buys you. So hopefully you can give it a test run in your next Cloud Code session. So here we are inside of the GSD GitHub repo. What we're going to do now is we're going to run through the repo so I can quickly explain big picture how the system works in conjunction with Cloud Code and then we'll actually hop into Cloud Code and play around with it for real. So first of all, how do we install this thing? Really easy. Just this one line of code in the terminal. MPX get [ __ ] done CC. Next question should be, well, how do I actually interact with this guy? Well, it uses slash commands. So, for example, if I wanted to start a new project with GSD, I would just do forward slashgsd new project. So, that's how you interact with it inside of cloud code. Now, you're probably asking, okay, how does it actually execute code? How does it take my idea and create a working application? Well, Tash, the creative GSD, breaks it down into six steps. Now, the first three steps are initialize project, discuss phase, and then the plan phase. Think of these first three steps as essentially your standard clawed plan mode on steroids. So, at the beginning, what do you do in the initialized project stage? Well, you just give it your idea. It's going to ask you questions. It's going to do research on your behalf. And this is where you're getting your like PRD, right? Your product requirements document. What is it you're actually trying to build big picture. And in this space, it creates four documents that have to do with sort of context. So Claude always knows what you want to build, what the technical requirements are, and then what has it created and what still needs to be created. And I will show these in action in a little bit. After that, you move into the discuss phase and the plan phase. And this is where you go from your general PRD and you get really really detailed with like, okay, but what do you actually want? So it's going to check the plan against the requirements in loop until they pass. Right? Starting to see some Ralph lloop fundamentals at work there. And so once we've planned everything out then we go into the execution phase. Now this is where again we see a lot of Ralph flu fundamentals because what's going to be happening here? Well, it's going to run plans and waves in parallel when possible. But really what you see is a fresh context per plan. Now you should probably understand at this point what context rot is. It's the idea that as a context window fills up right the more we interact cloud code the worse the outputs get. And autocompact only solves that so much. So what this does and what also Ralph loop does is it starts a new session in the form of a sub agent in GSD's case to actually execute the code to create the code. So for atomic task one, it's going to spawn a new sub aent. That sub aent is going to have fresh context to do it so we get the best output possible. And it does this for every single task. And each task also gets its own commit. From there, it moves on to step five where it's going to test to make sure the code actually works. And lastly, step six, we just repeat this process over and over until we get what we want. And so the GSD loop essentially becomes discuss, plan, execute, and verify.
