Now, the last thing I want to talk about is troubleshooting because I think troubleshooting is knowing how to properly troubleshoot is actually one of the most important skills you can have and one most people don't have. Instead, they usually just throw their hands up in the air and then, you know, they just kind of give up. But if you know how to troubleshoot, frankly, you can build anything because the AI systems like Claude and Gemini are getting so good when it comes to helping you work through certain end workflows, which means you can build whatever you want. So, what do we do when something goes wrong? How do we actually solve this problem? So, let's go back to this big automation as example. I had an error here. Okay. Now, over time, you'll get enough experience to be able to just go in here and figure out what exactly went wrong. But that's not always obvious, especially when you're just getting started. So, what should you do? Well, it's kind of a multi-part process. So, what you want to do is you first want to find an AI that you're comfortable with. And for me, that is Gemini and Claude. And so, what I'm going to do at first is I'm going to give it full context of what's going wrong with my particular automation. And so to do that, what I want to do is I want to download the code for the automation. So what do I mean download the code? Remember this is a no code platform, but all these visuals are just an interface essentially for code. There's a bunch of code going on down here. So I can just go ahead come here to the top right and the three buttons. I can go to download and you'll see here it just downloaded the code for this agent. I'm then going to go to Gemini, go to claude, go to chatgpt, and I'm then going to put that code in there. So, I literally just paste it in. The next thing you're going to do is give AI the big picture context. What do I mean by that? I've given it the code. Now, I'm going to explain to the AI, hey, this is what I actually want to do with my system. Here's the data I start with. Here's my end goal. This shouldn't take more than a couple sentences. Also, feel free to just use voice. Like, what's the point of this automation? What are you actually trying to do? Okay. Next thing we're going to do is we go back to where we're getting our error. I will then go into the module that has the problem and I'm going to do two things. One, I'm going to copy all this errors, all these error messages, right? Come up right here. Just click copy that. Next, what I'm going to do is I'm going to take a screenshot of this page itself. I then go back to the AI system. I'll then say, "Hey, error message." I paste that in there. I then paste in the screenshot. Okay? And then I tell it what I think is going wrong in that module. So to sum that up again, I first give it all the code, right? And the big picture context. Here's everything that's going with my workflow. I then give it this specific error message. I give it a screenshot of the module in question. And then I give it a brief explanation of what that module was trying to do. What have I done by doing this? I have given AI what it lacks. AI lacks context. You on the other hand as a human being you have unlimited context. You understand everything that's happening like theoretically in the system what you're actually trying to do. The AI is extremely smart. It's smarter than you when it comes to coding and all these like intricacies, but it doesn't understand context. It doesn't know what you're actually trying to do. So remember what your job is in this sort of AI human relationship as when it comes to this stuff. That's to provide context. And by troubleshooting like this, giving it as much context as possible, you can solve virtually any problem. I guarantee you. Now, you're going to give it this. It's going to come back with a potential fix. You're going to try the fix. It may or may not work at first. If it doesn't work, you just repeat the last half, right? Put the error message back in. Add another screenshot and a short explanation. You don't have to do the big context more than once. It it understands that moving forward. But by doing that, that's how you're able to solve problems and troubleshoot. And frankly, if you know how to troubleshoot, you can kind of do anything inside of Nadn because people don't know how to troubleshoot. They just know how to copy paste templates and that's pretty much it. And that is actually where I'm going to leave you for this NAN essentials because while we burn through that and you might not think we actually touched on a lot, we touched on quite a bit. Just from this video, you know how to do the following. You understand what the hosting situation is, self-hosted versus localhosted versus cloud hosting. You understand how to set up an AI agent. You understand what a system prompt is. You understand how to connect a chat model, how to connect simple memory, and you understand the AI agent external tool API dynamic, right? And lastly, and arguably most importantly, you now know how to troubleshoot what error, whatever errors you run into, which means you can now start going out there and experimenting. Start experimenting and not feel like you're going to instantly hit a roadblock and have to give up, right? You now have enough tools to get started. Now, like I said, we ran through that. === The MCP Problem ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JEznT-qXiA4

Did we overhype MCP? MCP was launched a year ago and it was heralded as the new standard protocol for how AI agents and large language models interact with external systems. The hype was insane, but if you're someone who's been using MCP in production level AI systems since then, you understand it has a lot of limitations. And this week, Anthropic released an article talking about those limitations. namely the extreme heavy token load MCP servers have on AI Asian systems to the point where they start to break down. In this video, we are going to dive into that article by anthropic so you can understand what these token limitations really are, how you can fix them, and what it means for you, an actual AI practitioner, moving forward if you still want to use MCP. So, this is the article I alluded to. It's called Code Execution with MCP. It was published on the 4th, and I'll put a link to it down below so you can follow along. Now, this article talks about two things in particular. One, the issues with MCP, namely token bloat when we have multiple MCP servers attached to one agent. The second thing it talks about is what we can actually do about it, which hint has to do with code execution. Now, as a very brief reminder, what is MCP? Well, first of all, it stands for model context protocol, and it's essentially a way for large language models and AI agents to deal with external systems. Normally, we do that with API calls. What you see here is both a traditional AI agent in the green and an MCP agent in the red. They are both focused on using fire crawl. Firecrawl is a web app. It's a tool that lets us scrape the internet. And in this case, there are five firecrawl tools, right? We have the ability to map websites, scrape websites, crawl websites, extract websites, and get the status. Right? Fire crawl five tools. Traditionally, I would attach these to my AI agent as individual tools. Right? We have all five here. And I would have a description inside the system prompt saying this is what each tool does, right? The problem is a I have to manually connect every single tool. And then b inside of each tool and they're mirrored down here. I need to go inside there and I need to set up everything, right? I need to set up the schema. I need to set up the API key. I need to set up the description. There's like a ton of manual work required to set up each tool. Now compare that to MCP. Instead of having five tools right here and I have to understand how each of them works exactly, I instead just have a list tools and an execute tools option. Right? This means that all the fire crawl tools are listed here in the list tools option. Using MCP, my AI agent can now just go to the list tools server and say, "Hey, I want to use firecrawl. I've come to the firecrawl service. You tell me all the tools you have available and you tell me exactly how to use them. I, as the user, don't even need to know how that works. I don't need to set up anything. I'm just telling him to get all that information from MCP. And once it has all these tool descriptions and schemas at its disposal, it can then use execute tools to execute said tool. Again, this makes it way simpler for you, the end user, to use complicated tools like this. Right? Before you really need to understand how each and every one of them functions, set it all up. Now connect it to the MCP server and let your agent go forth and conquer. Right? That's what MCP really buys you as the end practitioner of these agents. So back to this article, what exactly is the problem? Again, I kind of understand MCP. We're talking about excessive token usage. How excessive don't we have great huge context windows? Sometimes a million tokens large if I'm using something like Gemini. Well, the answer is yes, but not exactly. So here I am inside of Claude code and this is where I want to demonstrate sort of this token bloat idea. So right now I have my AI agent that has three MCP servers attached to it. It has an NADN MCP server that you've seen me use in previous videos. It has a Playright MCP server which uses for browser automation and then we have a GitHub MCP. Only three MCP servers. Okay. And we're using Sonnet 4.5 which has a token a context window of 200,000 tokens. Okay, we have not done anything with this AI agent yet. It has we haven't given it a conversation. We haven't told it to do jack yet. If I bring up the context window right here, what do we see? We see that without us having done a single thing, we have already used 66% of our context window. And of that 66%, half of that is coming from MCP tools. That's a problem. First of all, why is the MCP tools taking up so much space? Well, if you look here, you see how I scroll down? These are all the tools associated with those MCP servers. Remember the firewall one I showed you how it was five? Well, these MCP servers have way more than five tools, and each of those have a certain amount of tokens associated with them. You put that all together and a third of our context window has already been eaten up just by essentially laying out all these MCP servers and their tools for the agent. And now you're probably saying, "Okay, sure. 2/3 of the context window has been eaten up. I still got a third left. We're good, right?" H not exactly. There has been a number of studies done on what is called context rot. Context rot essentially means that your first 10,000 tokens are not the same as your last 10,000 tokens. Namely, those first 10,000 tokens work way better than the last 10,000. That also applies for the first 20 and the last 20, the first 100,000 and the last 100,000. The more context window, the more of that window that's eaten up, the less effective these AI agents tend to be. Now, there's no specific number at which, hey, every time at 100,000 tokens, we fall off a cliff. It depends a on the model and b what we are having the model do. If it's rather complex task that we are giving it, it tends to fall off even quicker. All that to say is, hey, we're kind of behind the eightball and we haven't even started because of this MCP bloat, right? A third of our window is already gone. And why exactly is that happening? Well, it's because how MCP tools actually work. As stated here, most MCP clients load all tool definitions up front directly into the context, exposing them to the model using a direct tool calling syntax. That is exactly what you saw here, right? see all these tools, see all those tokens, it's all getting loaded up front, even if we don't use 99% of them. And so that's the problem with MCP. We can't effectively use multiple MCP servers within one AI agent, right? It just overloads the LLM. And if you want an even deeper illustration of that, they have a really cool graphic here inside the article itself showing how these tools and tool lists keep getting bounced around the system every time we're having to do anything. So that brings us to point two, which is what the heck can we actually do about this, right? If I can't use MCP as I normally would, what are my options? Do I just throw my hands up in the air and give up on MCP entirely? No. So what Anthropic suggests, and we should listen to Anthropic because remember they essentially invented MCP and that is to present MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls. They tell us that the agent can actually write code to interact with the MCP servers. This means the agent can just load the tools they need when they need them, right? It doesn't always need all the information about every tool available at all times which causes that token bloat. Anthropic goes on to tell us that there's a number of ways to do this. Mainly, we can use some sort of file tree structure or use something like cloud skills. Now, this can be kind of confusing. So, let's talk about like what this actually means for you. So, like I said, Enthropic suggests doing sort of a file tree system. The kind of analogy I like to use is essentially like a normal MCP server. The way we're normally doing it is imagine traditionally for MCP we're giving the AI agent this giant dictionary and inside of that dictionary is what you see here every single tool all the description all the schema and obviously that is a ton of read that's a ton of tokens but we give it to our agent we say hey never let go of that dictionary you're going to look at that dictionary anytime you do something suggests we then take that dictionary right we chop it up into a thousand different books each corresponding to a specific tool and we go put it next door and we create this library. Now, this is where the file system comes in. And you can see it kind of visually depicted here, right right at the top of the screen. That file system is essentially a map for the AI system saying, "Hey, if you need something, here's what's in the library next door. Here's where you can find the stuff. Go there and get what you need when you need it. I don't always need you to hold on to every single book and just have that take up your context window. Just don't do that at all. Just get what you need when you need it. And in fact, according to the article, this brings down the token usage by like 99%. Right? So, this essentially solves the issue of the token blow while we are still able to actually use all the MCP features. And you can see them talking about that here, right? The agent discovers tools by exploring the file system. You list the available servers, right? This reduces the token usage from 150,000 tokens to 2,000 tokens. And here inside of my file system, you can see here on the left, MCP file system, the servers, you have GitHub, NAND, play, write, and then it even has like an example usage file right here for the agent to look at and understand what it needs to do, right? And once it finds the tool it needs, it can then execute it and write code on the fly based on those scripts. And again, if all that was super confusing, just understand we now are transitioning to a use tools when you need them approach versus the traditional MCP approach, which is you have everything all the time, right? It just it's too heavy. And if you doubt Anthropic, Cloudflare themselves published similar findings. You can find the link here. And they essentially have the same exact idea. Now, Anthropic goes on to talk about the benefits of this code execution style and essentially is, hey, AI is really good at executing code and writing code. Why don't we just lean on that? And from there, they go on for another few paragraphs basically showing different examples of what this would look like in the real world. And they even then reference Claude skills, which is a great way to kind of bring in this sort of scripting into something like Cloud Code. Now, what does this actually mean? And I think Enthropic again does a great job here of kind of talking about like the pros and cons, okay? Because if we go back to this, right, this agent right here, this singular MCP, firecol MCP agent, is this going to suffer from bloat? Is this going to suffer from context rot? Probably not. A because it has a very small amount of tools, but even if you quadruple these tools, it's still just one MCP server. And the reality is, if you're using an AI system with one, maybe two MCP servers, even if they are heavy, you can get away with that. We saw tons of videos I've done in the past using the NAN MCP server to great effect. We had no issues there. But if you want to create an AI system that uses multiple MCPs, right, and has an extreme amount of potential tools at its disposal, this traditional system that everyone has been using in the past will not cut it. Right? This it's just like you saw here. This was just three MCP servers and a third of our context window is gone. And again, we're always fighting against the invisible context rot, which is also really, really hard to quantify. Like I said, there's no exact token number where it's like, up, it's starting to suck. You just don't know, right? It's extremely variable. And so, you don't want to be operating in these gray areas for too long. But this code execution system does introduce complexity and that can't just be handwaved away, right? We are having it write code. We having it execute code. we're doing extra steps to send it to that room next door to check take a look at the library, right? All that introduces complexity. All that introduces another link in the chain that can be broken and have things go wrong. So, you as the user have choices to make. How complex do you want your system to be? How many MCP servers do you really need? And based on those answers, that will dictate whether you continue to use MCP like we've normally used or if you need to start using some sort of file system and doing the code execution as shown in the article. So in summary, is MCP dead? No, definitely not. But is MCP the silver bullet? It kind of was, you know, hyped up to be in certain circles also no. Right? Just like any tool, you need to know what you're actually trying to accomplish and like what your restraints are. How flexible can you be? How many MCP servers do you really need? Right? You just need to approach things intelligently, right? You can't just blindly use MCP in a certain context every single time anymore. So, hopefully this video helped um kind of explain this article for you. Watch out for more videos coming out with like Claude Skills where we kind of show how exactly this really would work in in a real life example. Um, and as always, check out all the free resources in my school down below.

=== 10 n8n Nodes You MUST Master ===

I have built hundreds of AI agents and automations inside of NN over the last year. And during that time, I've realized that out of the thousands of nodes available to you, there are really only 10 that form the foundation of any functional workflow. But before I really understood that, I was probably in the same position you are right now, which is chasing over complicated workflows, downloading templates without understanding the fundamentals, which meant it was a huge slog and struggle to actually create my own custom workflows for myself. But once I really internalized how these 10 nodes worked and how I could implement them inside of my own agents, everything flowed much more smoothly. So, in today's video, I'm going to show you what those TED nodes are, how they work, and what they look like inside of functioning workflows. And as a bonus, I'm going to show you how you can get all the agents and automations that you see in today's video for free. So, with that, let's get started. So, here are the 10 nodes that we are going to be talking about split up into three sections. And starting from the bottom, we got our data section. Then, we got triggers. And then up top, which is where we're going to start, is the holy trinity. These are the three nodes that are the rock upon which you are going to build your AI agent church. If you don't understand anything else from this video, I need you to understand these three nodes and what they do. Because if you only knew how to use those, you could honestly create some fairly sophisticated AI agents. I mean, heck, you could do it all, which is the code node, right? That's the kind of power we're talking about here. And you must understand how these work or you'll never be able to build anything. So, starting from the beginning, we got the HTTP request node. Now, what does this do? Why do we care? This node is what allows us to actually talk to things outside of NADN, right? I have this AI automation, this AI agent, and I need data either to go somewhere or I need to pull it. An easy example is like what if you had an automation that dealt with your emails, right? You used Gmail. How does it talk with Gmail? It uses an API. Well, how does that API actually like work? Well, it works inside of an HTTP request module, right? This is the node that lets us talk to things outside of the system, right? And you can very clearly understand why that's so important. Now, I brought up Gmail. You might be saying, "Hey, Chase, like why do I need HTTP request nodes? I have a Gmail node right here, right? If I want to delete a message, I just use this node." Well, I hate to break it to you, but this Gmail node, for all intents and purposes, is just an HTTP request node with a fancy wrapper, right? Everything that's happening here can happen here. And the thing is, while we have the Gmail node and we have the OpenAI node and we have hundreds of nodes inside of here, there's thousands and thousands of thousands of places you may need to push data to or pull data from, which is why you need to know how to do it using an HTTP request node. So that's what it does. How do you use it? So looking inside the HTTP request node, we got a lot going on. Now the big things you want to look for are the method. Do we want to do post or do we want to do get? Those are the two you're going to see the most often. Get means just like it sounds like you're getting data, right? I'm trying to pull data in. Post, I'm pushing data out. URL, where is this going, right? And then things like authentication, query, and header parameters all kind of fall under the same, you know, umbrella. And that's you essentially proving that you are who you say you are, right? Oftent times this requires like an API key, but it always depends on the program you're trying to talk to. Now, here at the bottom, we have the body. Now, if I'm doing something like I'm just trying to download a file and it's a get request, oftentimes I don't need to send a body. But you'll see this very often where we have a bunch of code down here, right? A bunch of JavaScript. And that is essentially our encoded message, right? Everything above this is as if you're sending a letter to that external program saying here's your address. Here's who I am. And the body is the message, the content saying this is the data I want and this is the format in which I want it. Still with me? Now what does this look like in practice? I think it'll make a little more sense. So right here we have an automation that's for creating SOR 2 videos and um I know I tease getting all these templates at the beginning. And this is similar to HTTP requests where it's all about us getting data from some outside source, but instead of pulling it manually, that data from some outside place triggers the workflow. What do we mean by respond to web hook node? Well, that means the data will then get sent back to that original source, right? So as sort of an example, where's 11 labs? There's 11 labs. That's how that works. That's how web hooks work in action. Now, similar to HTTP requests, how do I know what the URL is going to be? Is there any weird stuff that I need to look for? Documentation, documentation, documentation. So, node number six is the call NN workflow tool. Just like HTTP request tools. This is all about calling some sort of outside source, but instead of an outside program, this is just an outside automation, right? Something that probably lives somewhere else. So, where you often see this is in like personal assistant agents, right? I have this AI agent. It has an email tool, but the email tool, right, isn't on that canvas. It's in a whole another workflow. So, alls I do is I give it a name. I give it a description. Hey, you call emails. This is your purpose. And then I map it to some sort of workflow, right? There's all these different workflows I can map it to. In this case, it's the email one. So, if I follow that, what do I see? Well, I actually see a whole another AI agent that has a bunch of email functionalities, right? All of this isn't inside here. Yet, this agent can use all this agent's functionalities by virtue of that call ended workflow tool. Now, what does this really buy by buy us? Why not just have all of these on the same AI agent? It allows me to be modular, right? It allows me to have this AI agent and connect it to whatever I want, right? It allows me to sort of like peacemeal my AI agents and I can attach it to any other automation I so choose, right? And so it's a nice little thing to have in your toolbox. And lastly, we have our data notes. Merge, edit, fields, accurate, and weight nodes. Now, people don't really talk about these, but they must be understood if you want to create workflows of any complexity, right? The hardest thing with any isn't understanding AI. It's really not. The hardest thing is managing data, right? Especially when you're dealing with clients who have disgusting data and it's not cleaned up and it's horrible. You're really going to have to rely on modules like these, right? So, what are they to do? When do we need to use them? Well, if we look at something like this, we can see a number of them in action. So, let's talk about the merge node first and foremost. What is the merge node doing big picture? Well, it's merging stuff. It's putting all the data in the same place. Yeah. Obviously, what it's really doing, though, is it's a stop sign. Okay. Right here, what do we have? We have essentially four funnels of data that we eventually want to put into these like, you know, AI systems over here on the right. And this merge node acts as a stop sign that's saying, "Hey, nobody gets to pass go and get dumped into these AI, you know, report generators until you've all finished collecting your data, right? So, I need the YouTube data. I need the Reddit data, the Twitter data, the web search data. I need all of it. And you guys all need to be in one place ready to go before we go any further." That's what the merge node is great for, right? And I think it's kind of easy to visualize here, but you will see some disgusting workflows some people do and it's all this data is all over the place. And in those cases, like a they should be simplified, but b often times they'll need to rely on a merge node to just make sure data is moving along together at the same place because even something as complicated as this, sometimes they get even way crazier and you can very easily get kind of lost, right? Especially when you're mapping to modules way down the line. Now, inside the merge node, you have the mode and the number of inputs. Number of inputs is self-explanatory, then you have append, combine, query, and choose branch. These are relatively self-explanatory, but the one you're most often is going to use is just append, right? If you just want this to act as a stop sign to get all the data ready to go, just keep it as append. If you want to combine, you can combine certain things with each other. So, like if I wanted some data from the first funnel and the second funnel to be combined, then I could do that here, right? But it can get kind of complicated for so for nine out of 10 use cases you're okay with append. Now that brings us to the second data node and that is the set fields node almost with the aggregate. So let's talk about set fields. What is happening here? So the set field nodes can really do two things. We can kind of just rename data or we can completely overwrite it. What you see here is the data just renamed. Right? This was called JSON.output. So it used to be called output. Now it's called X data. Now, why is that? Well, because all of these AI systems give you an output that's called output. So, we can't have output output output output. We have to rename those variables, right? So, this becomes YouTube data. This is Reddit data. This is X data, right? So, it just kind of acts as a way to, you know, reset the variable naming structure to keep our data clean. The other option you have is you can just rewrite the data entirely, right? So if the JSON output was a certain thing, write a certain value and I just wanted to change it, well I can quite literally overwrite it. The third data node we want to talk about is the aggregate node. Now what is aggregate do? Just like it sounds, you have a bunch of data, a bunch of different items, and I want to turn that into a single item. So if I look at the executions here, we'll be kind of a we'll be able to see this in action. So we'll copy this editor. So what do we have here? Remember we had this merge node, four funnels, each gives us an output of one item. We have four items popping out of here. Well, what am I trying to do endgame? Well, endgame, I want to send all that data to these LLM systems to either generate a report or come up with content ideas. But I want that report generated with all four items as context, right? I don't want one report for YouTube information or one report with Reddit information. I want the report with all the information. But if these things are as four items, right? It's just going to go into that system one at a time. I want to put all those items into a box and then give that box to the AI. That's where aggregate comes in, right? Because certain systems like these LLM systems, if it's just if this says 40 items instead of one item on that line, well then it's going to process item one, give you an output, process item two, give you an output. I want it all done at once. So that's where aggregate comes in. Anytime you want all of your data to be looked at simultaneously, use the aggregate node. Now, last but not least is the weight node. And I included this one because believe it or not, you can have the weight node do more things than just count to five. In fact, if you do on web hook call, you can use this as a web hook node. And let me explain why that's actually pretty useful. So imagine a scenario like this one where we are trying to create an AI video. This is a process that can take four, five, six minutes. It depends. We don't know. How do we deal with this? Well, oftentimes you will see the non-elegant solution of some sort of like pull, right? Where it waits for a minute. If it gets it, it continues. If it doesn't get it, it does a loop and waits another minute. There are more elegant solutions like what what you see here where instead we make that request, right? In this case, I'm making a request to Kai AI to create a video for me. That request then gets sent to this weight node. That weight node is then set up as a web hook. Right? So, as part of this request, you'll see down here, what did we send? We send a call back URL. This callback URL is part of this weight node. You'll see here the web hook URL will be generated at this runtime. Reference it with this variable. What that means is when we give our message to Kai AI, we essentially added our phone number to it. We're saying, "Hey, give us a call back when the video is done, right? And once you give me the call back, I will then forward it along to the rest of the automation." This is in contrast to what you see oftent times where there's like a loop node where you send him the message and then every minute you're knocking on the door saying, "Hey, are you done?" "Nope." "Okay, I'll come back in a minute." "Hey, are you done?" "Nope, come back in a minute." This instead just says, "Hey, make us the make us the video. Call me when you're done." Right? And that can be pretty useful and save you a lot of time when these things are done at scale. But of note, unfortunately, not every API supports some sort of callback URL. most do, but you will run into situations where this just can't be done. But in the event it can highly advise you use it. So that wraps up this video. I really mean it when I say that these 10 modules totally form the foundation for most workflows. Right? When you look at something complicated like this, you know, there's a lot going on, but what do you see? Schedule trigger. This is an HTTP node. This is an AI node. Aggregate set fields. HTTP request. Merge aggregate AI. HTTP require workflow with all these things. 90% of it it's it's just these. It's just these, right? So, if you understand these 10, it is way easier to like not get lost in the weeds with these complicated automations because when you really drill down to it, it's some combination of this, right? It really is. And once you understand that, you realize, hey, like I'm essentially just playing with Legos, right? and I only got like 10 Legos, but with those 10, I can build whatever I want, right? You just have to master these fundamentals. So, hope you enjoyed the video. As always, check out the school, the free one. Tons of resources there. We just added some new tiers if you're someone who wants to get a little bit more into like content creation or the agency stuff. Obviously, still have Chase AI Plus if you really want to dive in head first to the agency work. Um, so yeah, check them out. Let me know what you thought. And as always, I'll see you

It then goes into YouTube and identifies the top three videos in that same niche and gives us summaries. Finally, it gives us some quick hitters in terms of content based on all the data it has scraped in the last 24 hours. You might be wondering, hey, why do we have all these aggregate nodes? has like the thing you'll notice as you get more and more experienced with Naden and automations in general is the hardest part isn't like hey coming up with this crazy idea the hardest part is like how do I get data from the left side of this equation all the way to the right side of the equation so it looks nice and neat and stuff can actually process things in the manner of which I want it to and one of the ways you're going to learn how to do that well is by understanding when to use aggregate nodes when to use merge nodes right when to use split out nodes and the thing you have to want ask yourself is like how do I want these items to get pushed because think about what we're trying to do here, right? Higsfield just released Popcorn, which they are claiming is the next nano banana killer. Understand that when you're dealing with voice agents, you're always going to be walking the tight rope between how much work can it do versus how long is this going to take and how quick is it going to respond. Because remember, every time you have it do something, it's going to naden, which isn't going to another place, bringing it back to you to then have a call, right? And so there is always going to be a little bit of a lag. This is something they are constantly improving, but there are things you can do to alleviate it. And a lot of that is going to be inside of the system prompt and keeping it tight and making it very specific how you want those tools used. Because if you give it a crappy system prompt, then you're just like, "Hey, here's a bunch of tools. Good luck." Well, then that's just more brain power, more tokens that the large language model has to use to figure out what it even needs to do. === n8n's NEW Workflow Builder Instantly Creates AI Agents (full tutorial) ===

So, NADN finally did it and released their own prompt to workflow generator, meaning you can now log into NAND, describe what type of AI agent or automation you want to build and it builds it for you. Now, this is something that the NAND community has been asking for for a really long time. And up until now, the only way to do anything like this was to use a bunch of thirdparty tools like Cloud Code, Sonnet 4.5, and the NAND MCP server all in conjunction. And while that was a viable option, it required a little bit of technical know-how. So having the ability to just log into Naden, type in what you want and have it built all in one place is a huge boon for people who don't want to go through all the technical steps to get something like Cloud Code up and running. Now, while this option will be more convenient, will it be any good? Gives me a good idea of maybe if I was confused of how I would actually do this, what it should look like. If it can hit that mark, it's really good. That's the best you can get right now. This hits that mark. Frankly, it does. The only thing that competes with this right now is a Cloud Code Sonnet 4.5 and in an MCP server build where you are also, let's be frank, paying $100 to $200 a month for cloud code, right? So, you can't use this if you're self-hosting, but you also can't use the Cloud Code system unless you're paying this exorbitant amount of month, right? And for many people it's worth it. But if you don't fall into that camp, then this is the best option you got got to go from prompt to agent to go from prompt to automation, right? It's better than anything else out there. It crushes any sort of cloud, you know, project build. It crushes any sort of custom GPT. So this is really, really good. Now, if you're one of those people who both uses clawed code and is comfortable with it and uses the anything cloud and is wondering, hey, which of the two are better head-to-head? I think it just is going to be a case- by case basis, honestly. And I will say the credit system is kind of a drag on this, you know, like I only get 50 credits a month if I'm on, you know, the uh starter plan versus Claude Code. Like I can go back and forth with it for a long time and I can also sit there and I can plan things out. With this, it's kind of a oneshot, right? I'm going to give it the prompt and then it's going to see what it builds and then I can edit it afterwards. But if I edit it with the prompt again, that's a credit and I only get 50 of those. So I really have like 50 like back and forths I can go with versus with cloud code like we can go back and forth all day. I can send it out online and can look at other stuff um that I feed it and we can actually sit there and plan out what our workflow is going to look like before we build anything. So headtohead I would still give the nod to cloud code because of the um flexibility right and the overall throughput because I'm not limited by credits but again if you're not in that camp this is huge. Did we just witness the death of NADN? Because today, OpenAI, the biggest player in the AI space, just released agent builder, which for all intents and purposes is their version of NAND. And so to recap, once you have this set up and saved and set to active, then you're able to come in here, you know, you just put in your frame, put in the prompt, set your parameters, hit create, and it will automatically run it and populate for you here. That easy. And then last thing over here on the left, like I said, this will take you to the APIs you need. If you click on the template down here for resources, it will take you here. just hit copy base up top and that will allow you to download the air table template as well. Um I also have links to the Kai AI one 2.5 as well as a breakdown of the cost in case you forget. So that's it for this video guys. I think this one 2.5 I think all the AI video stuff in general is super exciting. I've been using Naden for over a year now and in this video I'm going to show you the top 10 tips and tricks that I wish I had known from the beginning as it would have saved me a ton of time now. I don't want to have to fill this out every single time to change one or two things. I just want to go in here and add stuff, you know, like little minutia. So, once you do that, you'll hit save and you'll see it reflected right here as if the option of MR was in the form itself. And that brings us to tip number two, which is pinning data. Now, when I save this, it automatically pinned it. And you can see that up here, this data is pinned for test evaluations. Now, pinning and unpinning data, very simple. Over here on the top right next to that pencil, we see this little tack. And if I click it, it's unpinned. And if I click it, it's pinned. Now, why would we ever want to pin data? Why do we care about this? Well, again, it goes to testing. So, now that this is pinned, if I were to hit execute workflow, it's going to automatically use that pin data. I'm not going to have to insert all that information back into the form. It just assumes it's already been done, right? And I can continue to do that for every single module I want. And pinning data is useful in a ton of scenarios. Take this workflow for example. This is a content creation workflow that creates AI videos. This module right here does it. So, I'm going to go ahead jump in here and pin the data. And let's say I was trying to test something downstream of this module, right? It takes a lot of time to create videos. It's also expensive. If I was trying to test something here, right, the final module of updating the record, do I want to go through this workflow every single time to make sure it works? No, of course not. That would take a ton of time and it would cost me a ton of money. If I instead pin this data, every time I run this workflow, it's just going to assume it was already created using this pin data that already occurred in the first place, right? So, I just had to run it once, pin it, or I could run it zero times and, you know, go ahead in here and edit it however I wish, right? you can see sort of the possibilities in testing phase with editing and pinning. So you should be constantly pinning data when you are creating workflows for the first time. It's going to save you a a ton of time and b a lot of money. So now let's roll into tip number three which is shortcuts. Now if I want to pin this, it's really annoying if I have to go in here and manually pin it, right? Well, there's a shortcut for that. If I just click on the module and hit P on my keyboard, unpins it. Pins it. Unpins it. Pins it. I can even drag all these, right? and pin them all. Or, you know, conversely, click them all with control and they're all selected and unpin them and pin them. And there's a ton of shortcuts on N9 you may not know about. I can click on this module, copy and paste it. I can also copy and paste it between workflows. So, I copy that update rows and I can come into another workflow, right? This one right here, paste it in there. Really easy. It's really modular. That's one of the best things about NN. Now, let's talk about tip number four. This is debug in editor. This is one of the most important ones to understand when we're talking about workflows that are already in production. You're using this yourself. Now tip number five, we're going to talk about import curl which is means we're talking about HTTP request. This is the most powerful node inside of any end. Now why do I say that? So h header content type application JSON. Nope, that's not it. This is it. This header authorization bear your API key. This is ignored by most people, but it's actually very important. So, there's a lot of things you can mess with here, and most of them are just turned off and never looked at. But there is this the onerror message. So, if you click this, you have three options. It defaults to stop workflow. That means if I was using this HTTP request module and it failed, the workflow just seizes up. It errors out. It stops. But I have more options. One, I can continue. So, instead of it just stopping, it instead just passes an error messages, an error message, but it continues on as if it worked. Notice how there's just kind of one bar here, one path it can go down. If I go to settings and I set this to continue using error output, you'll notice we have a second path we can go down. Well just go into that module go to settings go to error output and you would create something like this some sort of path it can go down in the event of an error. And this gives you way more control when it comes to your workflows. You're not just blindingly hit. So speaking of errors, that brings us to tip number eight, which is troubleshooting. And I think this is the most important tip out of all 10. So how do we troubleshoot? Let's go back to this module where we had our little error that we debugged in editor. Let me tell you of the messages I get across like every single platform. 90% of them and that is not an exaggeration are all the exact same message. How do I fix this error in my workflow? They come to me because I have a lot of experience and they think I just know how to solve it. Sometimes I do, but 80 90% of the time when they have a problem, I go through the exact same flow that I'm about to show you now. And this flow requires no expertise. It doesn't require you to be a, you know, software developer. Doesn't require anything, right? It just requires you to do context engineering. What is context engineering? That's just telling AI what the heck is going on. That's it. And so that's how you need to troubleshoot whenever you're stuck and really, really lost. And I'm going to show you how to do it step by step. So, let's use this one as as an example. This Google map scraper. Let's say I just couldn't figure out what the heck is going wrong with this module. The first thing I'm going to do is I'm going to give AI big context, right? Very high level 30,000 foot view of what's going on. And the way that I do that is simple. I'm just going to give it the entire JSON code for the workflow. So, I'm just going to go to download over here on the right. I'm then going to go into my um AI of choice. These days, it's something it's either Cloud or Gemini. And I'm going to go ahead and I'm going to put that JSON file into here. Right? After I do that, I'm then going to explain the workflow. Right? That's step one. Explain the workflow. Big picture. This is what the workflow is. This is what I wanted to do. This is the ultimate goal. Right? Let the AI know what's happening. Then what I'm going to do, because I'm not done, that's not enough. I'm then going to go into this module, open all this up, and then I'm going to take a screenshot of all this. This way it can see the input, it can see the module itself, and it can see the error, right? And I'm going to put that in there. And then I'm not done. I'm going to take this error message, copy that, and say, "Hey, error message, paste it in there." And then lastly, I'm going to give it the tactical context. What is tactical context? That's me saying, "Hey, this is the exact module that's having the problem. Here's what's happening to it. This is what I think's going on. Now, this is what I wanted to do. And what you've done doing that is you've given AI the full picture of what the heck's happening, right? Here's the total workflow. Here's how it should end. Here's this miniature module. Here's the problem it's having. And here's all the information you need. That's context engineering. That's how you solve problems with AI. And nowhere along that route did you need to like really know what's even going on, right? You could train a child to do that, but that's how you should troubleshoot because your job like kind of going meta here in the AI human being like equation is you provide context. The AI knows way more than you, but you have unlimited context. They quite literally have a window known as a context window, right? And yet most people approach troubleshooting like, uh, I got an error. Module not work. What's happening? And it's just garbage in, garbage out. And you wonder why your stuff doesn't work. Well, it's cuz you're troubleshooting wrong. So go through that process. big context, small context, and then let AI go to work. If you retain nothing else from this video, I hope you retain that. All right, tip number nine, weight nodes. How to use them properly. This is a huge pet peeve of mine of watching other creators on YouTube because they are very lazy when it comes to how they use weight nodes. Weight nodes should not always just be used as this stupid module that just counts down from some arbitrary time, right? Let me give you some context. So, look at this workflow. This is another content creation AI video workflow. What you see in this module right here is us sending a request to Kai AI saying, "Hey, make us that AI video." And then we have a wait node. And then we have this HTTP request where we go back to KAI Kai and we say, "Hey, is the video done?" And if they say yes, we download it. Now, we have this wait module here because these videos take a lot of time and you don't exactly know how long they're going to take. 4 minutes, 5 minutes, 6 minutes, who knows? But if we're too fast and we go over here and we run back to Kai and we say, "Hey, is our video done? Is our video done?" and they say no. Well, then this workflow just fails, right? And to solve that, we would have to create a whole loop over here where we just check it again and again and again until we get it. That's super ugly. So instead, we often use this wait node where it's like, okay, we know it will take at least well, at the max like 7 minutes, so we'll just wait 7 minutes. We'll know we'll be done and then we'll go get it. That works, but it's inefficient. What we should instead do is we should tell Kai Aai, hey, this is the video we want. Go make it. By the way, here's my call back number. hit me up when it's done. You know, tell me when the video is done and then I'll go get it. That's what you can actually do with weight modules. Yet, no one does it. Let me show you how. So, we click inside here. What do we see here? Resume. Most people do after a time interval. Like I said, stupid dummy module counts down from 5 minutes, 10 minutes, whatever. We can instead do an on web hook call. This means like I alluded to before, when we first talk to that external program, we say, "Hey, do this thing for me." If we have this weight node in here, we can pass them back essentially a mini web hook that is telling them just let us know when it's done and we'll come back to you. Okay? And that web hook is going to be seen as the execution.resume URL variable. What the heck does that mean? Well, if I click back into here and we go down to this JSON, you see this right here, the callback URL, this is what you pass inside your JSON payload. This is the same for every single payload, for every single weight node. This won't change this call back URL. This is the same and this is how you should approach your weight nodes when you're in this sort of like ask an external tool for help, wait for the problem to be solved and get it back. This is how you should approach it. This is a much more elegant solution instead of doing some hamfisted wait node than where we say wait 10 minutes and then we wait 10 minutes. We don't want to do that, right? We actually kind of want to pretend we know what we're doing. So that's weight nodes in a nutshell. We're just giving them our phone number to tell us when the work's done. Easy enough. Now number 10, the last tip on the list. are community nodes. Now, community nodes are essentially custom nodes that people outside of NAD have created to use inside of NADN. Think of them as like mods for NAND, right? === RIP Google Sheets (n8n Data Tables Tutorial) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A1O6ZDdJ0j4

Nitn just released data tables, which means you might be using Google Sheets a lot less moving forward. So, what exactly are data tables? Well, it's just what it sounds like. It's NN's internal table system. Think of the same Google Sheet spreadsheets you've been using before, but now you can host them internal to NN, which means they are way faster. And by way faster, I mean literally 100 times faster. This becomes super important both at scale and while we're prototyping. we can move data way quicker from point A to point B and get our stuff up and running. So, in this video, we're going to see if that 100x speed boost is actually real. I'm going to show you how to set it up on your NAD instance, whether you're on the cloud or self-hosted. We're then going to go through how you can set it up in an agent format. And lastly, I'm going to show you how to create a very simple workflow to take data that's currently sitting inside of Google Sheets and move it to the end data tables. So, no more waiting around. Let's just jump into it. So, just like I promised, we're going to start with the speed test. Up here, we got the reigning champ in the green Google Sheets versus our upandcomer and it end data tables. Now, these have the exact same data in their sheets. So, you can look at it right here with the data tables. It's just a few columns, order ID, order date, price per item, and comments. Nothing crazy, very simple. So, we're just going to hit execute workflow, and we're going to compare the times at the end. Okay, that was pretty quick. Let's see the actual times. And we're able to do this by pulling up the executions at the bottom. And I'll expand this a bit. So we're looking at this right here, right? So when I click on the sheets, we'll see that it was successful in 779 milliseconds. All right. Now, if we look at the data tables, 6 milliseconds. 6 milliseconds versus 779. Right? Quicker, more than 100 times faster, right? This is huge. So before we go any further, let me just answer the question that you are already asking yourself. Is data tables really a replacement for Google Sheets? And the answer is it depends, right? It depends on what sort of data you're using and in what manner. So if we're using data like this where it's more simplistic, there's just a few types that we need, right? It's string, it's boolean, it's daytime, it's numbers, this can work awesome, right? You saw how fast it is and you can see how much value we could get whether we're scaled up or we just need a prototype. Now, if you're doing things in Sheets that are more like the Excel arena, like it's rather complicated. We have a lot of tables, like they're all talking to one another. Sheets is still going to be the winner there, but having this internal data tables as a tool in your back pocket is huge now. And so, I think it's important to understand where that value lies. Now, with that being said, let me show you how to actually get it onto your NEN instance. So, if you're in the cloud, it's pretty easy. You're going to come to the toolbar on the left. You're going to hit admin panel. You're then going to go to manage and then you need to get up to the latest beta. So 113.2 is what's working now. 11.3.1 works as well. And if you're watching this later down the road, chances are it's already, you know, integrated in it. But for now, you need to at least be on the beta. Now, if you're self-hosted, it's going to be a little more complicated because we need you to dive inside the terminal and we need to pull a specific NAD image from Docker. And all you should have to do here is just connect your proper file AI count. So, now we've stitched them together. Now, we want to upscale them, right? Cuz normally it's to 1080p. Well, I want 4K. I want 60 fps. And again, by the way, um, if you want to look at the FFmpeg right here on the left, you can just click that link. Same thing as Miniax. It'll take you there so you can look at all the settings if you want. But here we go. Now, we're going to send the request to actually upscale our video. Now, this one actually takes a while. We have a switch node. This is just checking like, hey, did we get the URL or is it giving us an error message that says it's still in progress. So if we get a response back saying it has the video URL, that means it was a success. It was a success and we can continue on. If we get the error message saying it's still in progress, it just loops back around, right? So, we're just going to wait. And again, this can take up to 10 minutes. Then we have these two air table nodes, a search records and an update record. Why is there a search record and why don't we just go immediately to update record? Well, because we want to identify everything that was 4K standby and then we update all the ones that were 4K standby. What does that actually mean? Well, if you don't have that node, then all of these won't get updated, right? Because it's going to go from 4K standby to complete once it's done. And this makes sure it goes through and updates all of them. It doesn't just update one or two. So, we're just covering our bases with that. And lastly, you'll then get the 4K video here. Now, all three will be the same, right? It puts them into each of the individual um rows, but that's fine. From there, you can just click on it and it's going to be super stuttery a lot of the times, but um you can go ahead and download it. The file will be fairly large because it's, you know, 4K 60fps. It's going to be kind of like, you know, just like you saw there, kind of rubbery, like rubber bandandy. Um but yeah, that's pretty much it. So, that's the automation, guys. You are going to live and die by your first frame, your last frame, and your prompt. Now slashinit creates a new claude.md file with codebased documentation. And anytime you start a new project using claude code, one of the first things you want to do is slashinit. Now alls it's doing is it's creating essentially a text document that's going to essentially summarize everything that's been done to our codebase up until that point. That way, further down the line, if you start working on this project again or you leave it alone and you don't come back for weeks and weeks and weeks and you start cla back up, it knows where it needs to check to see what the heck has been going on and what it needs to do going forward. So if I do shift tab, I can cycle through these different modes and you see them down here in the terminal. So question mark for shortcuts, accept edits and planning mode. So what is the difference between all these? So planning mode is just what it sounds like. Well, that answer is going to come in two parts. The first part is GitHub and the second part is Verscell. Now, GitHub and Versell are essentially two sides of the same coin. === 2 Step AI Agency Blueprint: From Tutorial Hell to $5K Clients ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NiAAsZAi3p8

So, let me walk you through an imaginary scenario. You're someone who has watched hundreds of YouTube videos about AI agents and automations, and you're actually getting pretty good at it. But you're no closer today than you were at the very beginning at actually making some money with these skills. Sound familiar? Well, if we want to actually start making some money with AI, what are we supposed to do? Well, most people take the complete wrong approach. They fulfill the definition of insanity, and they just start over at the beginning. more tutorials, more agents, more automations, thinking, "Hey, if I reach this mythical level of AI expertise, all of a sudden, people are going to start giving me money." But that's not how it works. In fact, taking that approach is going to do way more harm than good, because there's two parts of the equation to making money with AI. First is the AI piece, and you just have to get good enough. You don't have to be a complete expert. And the second part is actually sourcing clients, which is a completely different skill set and one you should be spending more time on than the first. So in this video, we are going to define what good enough actually looks like. And then we're going to talk about the second part, sourcing clients, how to do it, what those funnels look like, so you can actually start capitalizing on these AI skills you already have. So if you're somebody who's trying to start their own AI agency, wants to actually make some money in the space, this video is for you. And let's get started. Take a look at this personal assistant AI agent. If I ask you to explain to me how this thing works and you can do that, then you know enough about AI to start selling AI solutions. Do you need to know how to build personal assistant AI agents? No, because nobody buys these things no matter what YouTube tells you. But what you do need to know is sort of the fundamentals, right? And actually personal assistants are a great job of showing that. What do I mean? First of all, you need to understand how APIs work, right? because every single client's going to have some sort of API that they want put into an AI agent and it's going to differ from API to API. But if you can look at this and I say, "Hey, here's a Tavly API. Can you explain this to me? How do I get the key? How do I know what to put for the URL? How do I get all this information? How do I change what the API does? And where would I look to find that information, aka documentation?" If you understand that process, you know enough about APIs. If you can look at this AI agent and tell me, hey, here's how it interacts with the chat model, and here's how it interacts with its different tools, including sub agents, via a system prompt, then you know enough about how AI agents work under the hood to sell AI agents. I'm not joking. That's pretty much the technical bar you need to be able to sell these things. And if you don't know that, well, first of all, I would take a look at my 5h hour NAD master class like get the AI fundamentals. If you go through that, you honestly know enough. And if you're like, well, where do I actually go to find these things? For me personally, that would be perplexity. Right? You have to be able to know where to go when you don't know something. Right? That is the technical bar you must pass to be ready to sell AI agents. That's it. Nothing more. It's not crazy. You're probably already there. And so, like we talked about in the beginning, the first half of this making money in AI piece, the AI expertise stuff, it's not that hard. It's not too crazy and you're probably already there. Can you look at this? Can you explain the relationships? And you know how to tweak it to get it to where a client would pay you for it. If you can do that, you're ready to go. So, that brings us to part two, which is sourcing clients, right? Nobody cares how good you are at creating AI automations if they don't even know you exist. So, how do we get them to know you exist? Well, there's four main funnels. First is your warm network. Second is cold outreach. Third is freelancing websites. And fourth is social media. All four of these pay off at different speeds. They require a different amount of work upfront. But the big thing is you need to do all four of these simultaneously at the beginning because a volume is your friend. B you're going to suck at all of them. So, you need to start practicing. And see, you have no idea which ones will work for you in particular. And so, you have to cast a very wide net. And then over time, as you figure out what works, then you double down on it. You can't start doubling down on social media before you've done a single post. You can't start doubling down on freelancing websites if you haven't even tried cold outreach or reach out to your warm network, right? You got to try them all, which is why it's so much work at the beginning, but it pays off in dividends if you actually commit. So, first things first, your warm network. We're thinking like LinkedIn. and we're thinking our friends, family, co-workers, someone you've actually talked to in real life and actually cares about you. The big thing with the warm network is this. It's not a hard sell. You're literally going to talk to your friends and family and you're going to say something along the lines of just like, "Hey, I'm just starting this new business. I'm starting this new AI agency. This is what it's about. Do you know anybody who would be interested?" That's it. That's as hard of a sell as you do. Right? Minimum you're sending out a thousand a month. And you need some sort of way to write good copy, which is the actual emails themselves. How do you make it sound like each email was a one for one? Who you are, what you're about, why they should care, and why you're right for the job. That is something, like all these skills, you are going to suck at really badly at the beginning. And this is a place where you can practice that at scale at a low risk. And last on the list is social media. It's going to have the longest burn of all these. It's going to take the most amount of work up front before you see any sort of ROI from social media. === Firecrawl v2 MCP + n8n = INCREDIBLE Web Scraping AI Agent (No Code Tutorial) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DrFKn9BiWCE

Firecrawl is one of the best tools out there for scraping the web and turning it into data that you can actually use with your large language models. And this week, Firecrawl just announced version two of their software, which they claim scrapes web pages 100 times faster than before. So, in today's video, I'm not only going to show you how Firewall works inside of NADN, but we're also going to dive into the Firewall MCP server. So, I'm going to show you how to set up the MCP tools, what the pros and cons are of going down the MCP route, and then we'll do a use case where we are looking at our competitors Amazon web pages and seeing how their products are doing on a day-to-day basis. I'm actually really excited about this lesson because there's a ton of use cases when it comes to firecrawl and web scraping in general inside of nn and it's great to have a tool that lets us get outside the ampify box we usually play in. So, if you're ready, let's dive into it. All right, here we are on firecrawl and let's talk big picture about what this tool actually does. So, as reductive as possible, it's a web scraping tool. You give it websites, you give it URLs, it will go there, it will scrape the data, it will turn it down into markdown, which you can then give to your large language models for it to use. What's the most obvious use case here? Let's say you were building a chatbot for a website and you wanted it to be trained on other websites or your own. Well, you just give it the URL. It gets all the data for you and then you give that data to the chatbot. That easy. But if we want to go another layer deep, there's about four different things that Firecrawl is able to do for you. And the four big things that Firecrol is able to do for you is reflected here in these modules in the purple. You can map a website, you can scrape a URL, you can crawl a website, you can extract data. Now, why do we care about this? Well, it's important to know what's going on under the hood when we start working with MCP. So, you have some clue what the AI is doing for you and why. So, first map a website and get URLs. I can give firecrawl say like a URL like naden and then it will grab all of the URLs associated with that website which is what you see here. I gave it naden.io and there are 4,652 URLs associated with that website as you go layer by layer by layer by layer. Quite a lot, right? Well, that's what this does. And it's important oftent times to map a website so you can scrape the appropriate pages. Next, we have scrape a URL and get its contents. This is for a single page only. So let's say out of this 4600 grouping of NAND web pages, I knew exactly which one I wanted to scrape, right? Best AI for coding, for example, on this blog. Well, I would give it that URL and it would scrape all the data from that singular page. That's what scrape does. Next, you have the ability to crawl a website. Now, crawling the website means it will essentially scrape multiple pages. It will go layer by layer by layer as deep as you want it to go, and it will get more and more and more data. So just think of scraping but deeper and multiple iterations. Next we have extract data. And extract data is just a little more agentic for scraping and crawling. When it goes onto a web page, it grabs everything. For example, I had it scrape that page we were looking at earlier, right? The blog nm best AI for coding. This is the page in markdown. You know the the format that LLM's really like. It'll also give it to you in JSON, but it gives me everything on the page. This is quite a bit. The difference with extract is I can give extract a prompt and I can also give it special schema instructions where I tell it exactly what I want it to grab from each page. So it won't grab everything. It will intelligently just grab the stuff I give it. Now if we look inside extract data, you can kind of see that reflected here in the schema. Notice how I tell it, hey, I want the title, price, star rating, total reviews. This is in reference, and you'll see this later, to having it scrape or having it extract a specific Amazon web page. And there were specific things I wanted to get from that Amazon web page. Title, price, star, total reviews, right? I have the ability while using the extract data tool to specify that I don't want everything. I just want a few things. This is what they are. So those are the big four tools when it comes to firecrawl. Map, hey, give me all the URLs associated with this website. scrape. Give me everything on this one page. Crawl, give me everything on a number of pages and then extract. Give me specific information on these particular pages. That's the difference. That's what firecrawl can give you. Now, zooming out, we talked about MCP, right? We talked about how this was going to be a little MCP lesson. So, let's dive into that piece. So, real quick, little refresher. What is MCP? Model context protocol. We could dive into this for 30, 45, 60 minutes. Big picture, what is MCP buying us in the context of NADN? Well, what it means is we go from here, the traditional AI agent, what you see here in green to the MCP agent, what you see in red. What's the difference? It's the tools. Notice how up here in the green, I have a specific tool for each specific, sorry, I have a specific module for each specific tool, right? I have the firecraw map tool, the scrape tool, the crawl tool, blah blah blah blah blah. Every tool has an associated module that this AI agent must know and call specifically, right? Every tool must be listed. Imagine if this was Gmail, right? You would have one for send message. You'd have one for delete message, draft, tag. All the tools would be listed. What does MCP buy us? Well, gives us this. We just have two tools. We have the list tool and we have the execute tool. What MCP allows us to do is when we tell this AI agent to do something, it is first going to go to the list tools option. If we click in here, what do we see? I'll move this over here. We see an entire list of all the tools available inside of the MCP server. Do you want to guess what all this stuff is saying? It's saying, "Hey, here's scrape. Hey, here's extract. Hey, here's map." It's giving it a list of all the tools so it can say, "Hm, what's actually available? what can I use? And it's all housed in one singular module, right? So, it's essentially stuffed all five of these into this one. So, every time we talk to this AI agent, it's first going to go to that list tools and say, "Hey, what's available? What are my options here?" From there, once it gets the list of all the options, and let me put this back. The AI will do it automatically. That's really the power of MCP. It's the fact that the AI now knows, okay, these are the tools available to me. This is how I need to set them up. Let me just do it on my own. There's no more like, okay, we got to go in here. I need to set up the schema properly. I do all all this is gone. All this stuff is gone. AI just does it. It just does it. So what does that bias? It buys us a ton of flexibility. It means I just talked to this MCP firecrawl server like I would a human being and it's going to act like one. But that flexibility comes at a cost and the cost is precision because I will prompt this as best as I can, but at the end of the day it's going to be given a list of six, seven tools and it's just going to have to choose the best one it thinks for the job, right? versus if I am very specific and I just have certain modules ready to go, right? I could create an automation with just a few modules that are always going to execute the correct one every single time. Kind of just depends on your use case, right? If you're doing something a little more agentic, you need some more flexibility, MCP is great. === The ULTIMATE n8n Agentic RAG System (SQL + GraphRAG) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BTxghC1qHbw

How can we create a rag AI agent that can actually deal with our large Excel files? This is a question that doesn't really have a simple answer. And it's one that's overlooked in the vast majority of RAG tutorials that you see all over YouTube or anywhere else. And the reason for that is because you can't treat large Excel files the same way you do PDFs or Word docs when you're creating vector databases for most RAG ecosystems. It's not as simple as chunking up the data, turning it into a vector, and going from there. Because these large Excel files or Google Sheets are relational, they need full context. We can't just chop it up halfway through and expect our AI agent to give us correct answers. So, what do we do? Well, most people just ignore it. But in this video, I'm going to show you a rag AI agent that I believe solves this problem. This agent allows us to use Excel files inside of our rag system because it doesn't treat them as Excel files. It sends them to Superbase and turns them into proper database structures using Postgress. And by doing that, we're able to ask it questions and get accurate answers because we don't rely on vectorization. We're instead going to rely on SQL. But for you, the user, it all works the same. You just ask your agent a question in natural language, and you're going to get an answer back. So, in this video, I'm not only going to give you the template for this agent, but I'm going to explain how it all works, why certain decisions were made, and what you need to do to turn it into your own. So, with that, let's jump into it. All right, so here's a look at our AI agent. Now, the way this video is going to go is I'm going to kind of break down all the different sections of this agent so you understand what's going on big picture-wise and what to expect for the rest of the video. Naive rag, traditional rag, how does it work and why does it not work for Excel files? Why does this video even need to exist? Well, remember when we are creating a rag system? How does it usually work? You have a bunch of documents. Each document then becomes chunked up. If I have a thousandpage document, it does not go into the vector database as a thousandpage document. Instead, it actually becomes like 10,000 little chunks, right? So, imagine this page getting into three different chunks, right? Chunk one, chunk two, chunk three. Works great in text. From there, what happens? Well, each chunk becomes a embedding, right? So, this chunk becomes an embedding. And embedding is just a mathematical like equation essentially. It's a bunch of numbers. We're taking text and we're turning into numbers. From there, what happens? Well, those numbers then become a vector. So what you see here is chunk 3 being depicted as a vector, right? Imagine this is like a three-dimensional graph. In reality, it's actually way more dimensions, but you see how we've gone from a document, one coherent thing, into a chunk into some math into a vector. And when you ask a question of your AI agent, that's a rag agent. How does it work? Well, your question also becomes an embedding. So imagine your question became this series of numbers. It then takes those numbers and it says, "hm, which of these vectors are closest to the question, right? These numbers, which one is closest?" Let's say says it's chunk three. It says, "Great. Oh, chunk three was closest to the question. That has to be the answer." It then takes chunk three and probably several other chunks near it. And then what does it do? It brings it back to your AI agent. Your AI agent takes all those chunks, does it AI magic, and says, "Hey, here's the answer." This works for text documents. Why? Because chunk three like key agenda items. If I ask for Q4 2023 finances, it can give me all this. It doesn't necessarily need all this information, right? So chunking works. Chunking does not work with Excel. Where do I chunk here? How can I chunk up this document so I get the right answer when I say, you know, hey, what was the last name of order ID 1017? How the heck do I make my rag AI agents more accurate? This is a question that you have 100% asked yourself if you spent any time at all building rag systems. And the answer that I propose for this question is graph rag. Now what is graph rag? Well, you know how traditional or naive rag works, right? You have your documents, it gets chunked up, it goes into embedding and goes into a vector database, right? Well, graph rag takes it a step further where we aren't just using a vector database. We're using a knowledge graph. And that knowledge graph is essentially taking that vector database but defining the relationships between each vector. And why do we care about that? Well, because it gives us more accurate answers, which solves our problem in part of how do we make rag systems that actually work. And yet, finding information about how to integrate graph into AI agents on platforms like NAN is really hard to find. So, in this video, I'm going to pull back the curtain on all this.
