Lastly, we're going to go inside of Naden and connect all those dots. So, that's how it's going to work. And let's start with the explanations. So, first naive rag. This is traditional rag. This is all the rag that you see on YouTube with anything creators, right? Where they're using superbase or pine cone. They're putting some sort of documents into it and then it's vectorzing it and then they ask questions about what's in the vector database. So, how does that work? A quick review over here on the left, what do we have? We have all of our documents, right? These are all the documents we're going to put into our vector database. Each document is then chunked, right? If I have a 100page document, it doesn't just put it all in as a 100page document into the vector database. It becomes like a,000 10,000 different chunks, right? This just makes it smaller and easier for the large language model to find what it needs to answer your questions. And the way that actually works is each chunk becomes an embedding, right? So chunk three, for example, becomes this embedding up here. And each embedding is just a bunch of numbers, right? Here just those three. It could be 10, 20, 30, a billion numbers, right? In theory. But it's just taking that chunk and turning it into some sort of mathematical figure. From there, the embeddings go into the vector database. So in this case, this embedding that was chunk three became this blue embedding right here, which is represented by man. Now pretend it says chunk three instead. So it goes in the vector here. This could be vector 2. This could be vector 1. This could be vector 4. Right? You kind of see what's happening here. It's taking the chunks and it's putting it onto a graph. Right? In this case, it's a three-dimensional graph. In reality, it's hundreds of dimensions. Now when I go to my AI agent and I ask it a question and I say hey tell me you know about let's say Q4's 2023 financial review but what happens is that question that query also becomes an embedding which gets sent to the vector database and then AI says hm which embedding is that question closest to in this case it would be closest to this one right this vector up here imagine this says chunk 3 my question had to do with Q4 financial data which was in chunk three. So it would go right here like all right that's the vector that it's closest to let's grab that and in fact it might grab 2 3 4 5 six other chunks that are close to that embedding and then it takes that embedding and takes all those embeddings right all those different vectors it found gives it to AI which then gives you your answer right that's rag it retrieves the vectors it augments that information to its answer then generates you an answer that's how vector databases work that's how traditional naive rag works and sometimes we put a spin on it. We put a reanker, right? We give it some bells and whistles, but that's how it works traditionally. Now, as an analogy, think of it as a library. Okay? Each book is a vector. Each vector is a chunk. And the AI in this case is the librarian. You're asking it, hey, tell me about Q4 financial data. And they're going to say, which book is closest to Q4 financial data? And it's going to bring it to you. Traditional rag. So, now let's talk about graph rag has a lot of similarities. We still chunk, we still embed, we still vectorize, but in parallel at the same time we are doing entity and relationship extraction. So as it's chunking up those documents and looking at what's inside, it's defining what are the entities in this document, what are the relationships between entities and then it's usually pulling those as triplets, right? So for example, look at this one right here. This chunk right here, you see we have a number of attendees. Each attendee is going to be considered an entity, right? And there's a relationship between all these different entities. One, they're all in this board meeting. Two, they all probably talked about the key agenda items. But you can see like there's a bunch of relationships you could pull just from the singular page. And the way it often pulls them is something akin to this, right? We have the entity, which is Sarah Chen. We have the relationship. She is the CEO of Chase AI. And it's usually pulled as a triplet which would be Sarah is CEO of Chase AI subject predicate object. And it does this over and over and over as it goes through the document. And as it does this it creates nodes and it creates edges. The entities become nodes and the relationships become edges. And so what does graph rag do with all that information? Well, it creates what you see here and that's a knowledge graph. So these circles, right, Alex Harrison for example, that is an entity aka a node. And the connections you see between the different circles, the dots, the nodes, those are edges. Those are the relationships between them. So that's what it builds. And so it's kind of simplistic, but imagine if we got rid of all those connections. Think of that as vectors. Think of that as just a vector database. And with a graph rag, we're now making connections between them, which allows the AI to give us more insight when we actually ask it questions. And so all that leads to this which is increased performance. Now this graph comes from light rag. So it shows them just killing it. They're even whooping Microsoft's graph rag which is a pretty robust graph rag system. But big picture what are we seeing here? We're just seeing better performance. Right? We are getting something tangible from these relationships that are built and that's better accuracy. But this performance does come at a cost. Literally it will cost you more money to run graph rag systems. Right? We get the pros answer accuracy context. It's a better product. But in terms of cost, it's there. For example, I shoved in all the Lord of the Rings, like all three books, which comes out to like a,200 page PDF. That cost me from embeddings alone about $8. And that was using GBT5 Nano. So that's a very cheap model, and it's pretty fast. But in terms of speed, it also took it about an hour and a half to do that. So 1,200 pages becomes $8 and an hour and a half of your time. Understand that going in. All right, that's enough theory. Let's actually build something finally. There's a million different models. How are you supposed to know which one to choose? Well, each model costs a different amount because it uses a different amount of power. And the way that price is calculated is based on tokens. Think of every word that you send GPT as an individual token. And the price for every model is charged by millions of tokens. So if we look at OpenAI's pricing right here, right? If we look at 4.1 mini, this is breaking it down into input, cache, input, and output. So input, what is it saying? It's saying for every 1 million tokens, think of if I give this model 1 million words to look at. I have a 1 millionword query, it's going to cost me 40 cents. And it's also going to cost money for the output. So if I give it a $1 million query for 40 and it came back and gave me a 1 millionword response, that would be $160. So that's how the pricing is done for these different models. And you can see it's different for each of these, right? 4.1, $2,8.1 Nano, even cheaper, 10 cents, and 40. So there's always kind of this balance that you have to strike between how powerful of a model do I need and how much am I willing to pay. But also realize 40 cents for a million tokens, that's a lot of tokens. That's a lot of words. So fairly cheap in the grand scheme of things. And if you're ever confused about, well, which model should I choose for what, I suggest just asking chat GPT. So for this, we're just going to use 4.1 Mini, which is one I really like. Well, if you click inside your AI agent and go to system message, you'll see it says your helpful assistant. Now a system message is just the internal set of instructions that every sort of AI agent checks with before it answers you. Right? This is the logic. This is sort of the training that you're giving it. Now, chat GPT has one under the hood. You don't see it. And without any further ado, let's get started. So, first of all, what is an AI agent and what makes it differ from just a basic AI automation? Well, what we're looking at here is essentially like a very soft clone of chat GPT, right? It has a model. We can talk to it and can remember our conversations. And you saw that in the last lesson. But what you'll notice right here is tools. And tools and how an AI uses those tools are what makes an AI agent an AI agent. For example, if I click on this tool button here, you will now see on the right a list of a ton of different tools that are native to NADN, right? We see a bunch of stuff including Google, right? Google Calendar, Google Chat, Google Firestone, Google Docs, right? Google Drive for example, a million different things. And these tools can be attached to our AI agent and they can actually use these tools on our behalf. So, let's do a simple one. Let's say I wanted to add Wikipedia. Once it's been added, it's automatically connected to the AI agent and that AI agent now has the ability to essentially call on Wikipedia and answer questions about it. So, we can go and chat and say, "Hey, use the Wikipedia node and tell me about AI agents." And so, we should see here that it should call on this Wikipedia tool. And that's what happened right away. So instead of just relying on its training data to answer us like it normally would, it actually went to Wikipedia, figured out what the article said about AI agents, and then spit that out as a response. But one tool isn't really enough. We would want to add more tools. So like I could add a Google Drive tool, right? And then I could add something like air table, you know, and I could just continue adding tool after tool after tool and eventually have just a very large toolbox that my AI agent has access to. And with those tools, I can then tell the AI agent to do a number of different things. And it's going to intelligently decide on the best tool to use for the job. And those tools could themselves include AI agents, right? You could have AI agents talking to AI agents talking to AI agents all the way down the stack. But the thing that makes it agentic is that it doesn't always do the same thing every time, right? The way it chooses its tools and how it executes the job is dynamic. And it all depends on what you tell it to do. Now, compare that to an AI automation where it always does the same thing every single time, right? It's kind of just linear. It's straight line. And it could be, hey, every single time you get a mention in Slack, I want you to, I don't know, send an email to me. A happens and B happens and C happens. It's not dynamic. You're never wondering like which tools it going to use, right? Linear. Linear is for automations. Dynamic is for agents. Now, if that's kind of confusing, that's totally okay. It'll make more sense as you see some practical applications. Just remember, AI agents usually means lots of tools, dynamic. And what you will also notice is when it comes to tools and actually using them inside N&N, we usually need to hook them up like we did in part one. Hey everyone, welcome to part three of my NAND master class series where I take you from someone who has no clue what's going on in this low code AI space and turn you into somebody who can actually make AI agents, speak intelligently about them, and even sell them. So, if this is your first time jumping into the series, I suggest checking out parts one and two because each video builds upon the other and we start at a very foundational level. So, if you have no real technical background, you're not really sure what's going on inside of Benaden, just start at the beginning. But if you're a little more advanced and you want to hop in right away, go ahead. Because today, what we are going to be learning about is HTTP requests. And HTTP requests are probably the most important module that you need to wrap your head around because HTTP requests and really API calls in general are one of the most foundational tools that you really need to internalize how they work and how to apply them if you want to create robust AI agents. So let's start with diving into APIs. Now what are APIs? Well, it stands for application programming interface and it's just a fancy way for saying this is how two programs talk to one another. So let's imagine we have two programs. Let's say we have NAND on the left and OpenAI on the right. So I'm inside NAND and I want to use OpenAI's GPT4 to do something for me. Well, how do we do that? Well, we do it with an API call. So I send a message to OpenAI and included in that message is my API key, which is essentially like a password saying, "Hey, this is who I am. This is verification of what I want that I have billing. Please do the task for me." That is an API call. OpenAI gets your request, it verifies your key, and then it sends you that information back. That is an API in action. And anytime you are having a third-party program do something via an API, that's called an API call, right? So, we're calling that's an API call. We're calling on them. That's all that means. You're trying to contact some sort of third party program to do something on your behalf. And the way you send that data is via an API call. And as I said before, we did that here in OpenAI. And these things we did last time in part two where we uploaded files to a Google Drive and where we sent a draft. That was also an API call. And the nice thing is is these are actually set up very intuitively for us, right? I go inside here. I don't have to write any code. I have these boxes that I just do drop downs for, right? My resources, my operation, the email type, the subject, right? It's very, very intuitive of what information I put where. But if you were coding this, it would actually be an entire code block instead. And when I hit plus on the toolbox, you can see over here on the right, and I'll move my thing over. We have so many different thirdparty tools we can connect to. And when we use them, those are all using API calls as well. Now, some of them, like Wikipedia, don't even require an API key. They'll just let you freely use their resources. But most of these have a corresponding API key like we saw with OpenAI. And luckily, most of them don't require such a laborious setup process with their credentials like Google did in part two. And while there is a ton of tools here, the question you should be asking yourself now is, well, what if those tool doesn't exist inside of Naden? Because you just saw, I don't know, a 100 tools, 200 tools, but there's millions of programs on the web that you probably want access to and probably want their data. How do you send an API request to them if their tool doesn't exist? Great question, and the answer is an HTTP request tool. Now backing out for a second like we saw here these are all like customized right the Google draft right you got the operation the email type the options the actual message right very very easy to use each of these modules inside of n that's has their own special tool like Gmail like openai think of that as like a custom letterhead or a custom form that Google has given us that has given n that says hey every time you want to talk to me fill out this exact form in this way this is the Google form right super Easy. And to contrast that, the HTTP request is essentially like a boilerplate generic form that we are going to send to any third party that we want to make an API call to that already doesn't have a special node inside of NADN. And because of that, understanding how HTTP requests work is extremely important because in the grand scheme of things, most APIs will not be found inside of NADN, right? A lot of the really popular ones are like Google and all that stuff, but there's going to be a ton that aren't. and you need to understand how to navigate HTTP requests and that's what we're going to do today. So my suggestion is whenever you have the chance to break up these workflows into separate structures and different workflows and have them talk to them that way. Do it right. Hey everybody and welcome to part five of my NAND master class where we take you from someone who has no idea what's going on inside of NAND and turn you into somebody who can actually make sophisticated AI agents and even sell them. Now, if you remember in part four, we dove into sub agents, right? We built our own content agent in an entirely different workflow and had these two AI agents talk to one another. Well, in part five, we're going to be stepping up our game a little bit and going into RAG, retrieval augmented generation. And I'm really excited about this one because I think rag is one of the most important tools that you need to wrap your head around if you really want to build robust AI agents. As someone who actually builds and sells AI agents for a living, rag agents are the most popular AI workflow that I sell and I would argue it actually brings people the most value. So, here's the most recent RAG agent that I sold to a client. I actually gave it to him last week, ended up selling it for $5,000. And you can check out one of my previous videos for a full deep dive of how this works and how to set it up yourself. And obviously I even give away the template for free. But I wanted to show this so we can kind of talk about what rag actually is and what we need to do to create it because we're just going to create a very simple version for today, right? Like we're all about the basics, all about the fundamentals in this video series and we need to understand how it works at a very base level before we can upgrade and start doing some varsity stuff like this. So first things first, what is rag retrieval augmented generation? Now, when you talk to Chad GPT, what information is it using to answer your questions? Well, it's relying on its training data, right? It's been trained on millions and millions and millions and millions, trillions of parameters to answer your questions. But what if I wanted to answer questions that are specific about me or my team or the project I'm working on? What would you do? Well, you would go ahead and you would dump some files into it, right? Like you would put in an Excel file, you would put in a PDF, you would give it documents for it to answer questions about. That is essentially a very simplistic version of rag. So rag means it's going to retrieve a document from some sort of database. It's going to augment its answer based on the documentation and then it's going to generate an answer for you. Rag. Now in this one you're looking at the database it goes to is being is living inside a superbase. And the type of database that's being used is a vector database. Now, I'm not going to go super in deep into vector databases and all the math behind it and highdimensional stuff. Just think of it as a special database that AIs and large language models love because it's easy for them to grab information from there. That's all you really need to understand. Now, the other part you need to understand is how do we get documents from wherever they are, whether it's a Google Drive or on your computer into a vector database that the large language model that our AI agent can actually use. Well, you need some sort of ingestion system and that's what you see going on here in the red. Now the long story short of this is we start inside of a Google drive. These documents then get tagged. They get a bunch of metadata sent to them. We extract the text and then it's put into our vector database. Now one thing of note when going inside a vector database the way it works is imagine I had a 100page PDF. When it goes into the database it doesn't sit there as a 100page document, right? That would be a lot of text for it to constantly go through to find the answer. Instead what happens is it gets chunked. And you kind of can see that here at the bottom with the character text splitter. Basically, every 500, 750, 1,000 characters, that document gets chunked up into little bite-sized pieces that then all go into the vector database. And because it's in small pieces, it's easier and it's quicker for the AI to be able to find the text we need to answer our question. So, it's a speed thing. So, that is something you need to know because it does have its pros and cons, right? On one hand, we get speed, but sometimes it gives us a loss of accuracy, right? You can start thinking about well how is it going to know the right answer if I shove a giant Excel file into there that's thousands of rows and hundreds of columns like if it's chunked up how does it actually have context everything great question and you are just starting to get into the deep waters that is rag and some of the intricacies with it but for today we're just going to stick with the basics which is just like Google Docs and PDFs and putting information into a database and then having AI retrieve it right once you get your hands on that then you can start trying to do some uh highle stuff with like large Excel files and putting in SQL, right? It goes it goes kind of deep, but I say all that just to let you understand what's kind of going on under the hood, right? We're going to have some documents that we want our AI to know about. We need to put them into a database. And so, we need some sort of ingestion system. Once the documents are inside the database, then we just need to add some sort of tool to call on it. And that's what you see here in the Superbase vector store, right? It's now going to be it. we now just gave it a tool that says, "Hey, go call on that database whenever I ask you a question about X, Y, or Z, right?" And that's what it's going to do. And then we we might even go into some reranker stuff as well. We probably will. We'll see how we're doing on time. Um, but that's what we're going to be doing in a nutshell. We're going to be building an ingestion system for Rag and then we're actually going to have our AI agent call on that rag to answer questions. And this is super important, right? It be it should be kind of obvious as to why you should care about this, right? You can now essentially take chat GPT, you can take your AI agent, you can plug it into your business, into your project, into your life, and it's going to be able to do things that aren't just limited to it training data, right? It's going to know about your documents, going to know about your project, and can actually act as a member of your team and drive a ton of value that way. And if you're someone who wants to sell these sorts of things, almost everybody wants some sort of rag element inside of their AI agent, right? So, you really need to like understand how this works and know how to implement it. And that's what we're going to do today. Okay, so here we are with our orchestrator and we are going to be adding a rag system to it. Both the ingestion and the ability to actually answer questions based on the documents inside. Now there's two big thirdparty applications that you will see people use for rag in the context of edit. It's going to be superbase and pine cone. The last example you saw superbase and today's we're going to do pine cone because I think it's a little more beginner friendly and easier to understand but the fundamentals between the two of them remain the same. And for the ingestion system we are going to be using Google Drive. So, we are going to be putting files into a special rag folder inside of Google Drive, which is going to go into our Pine Cone database, which our AI agent will then be able to query. So, that's what we're going to do today. So, first things first, we are going to go to Pine Cone and create an account. So, you're going to go to pine cone.io. You are going to sign up. Go ahead, continue with Google or whatever you want. And you should see a page that looks like this. If you don't see it, go to the left, go to databases, and go to indexes. And so, we are going to create an index. So create index. Call it whatever you want. I'm just going to call mine, we'll call mine part five. For the configuration, you know, we have all these different configurations. What you're going to do is you're going to do text embedding 3 small. Just click that. We're going to scroll down. You can keep it serverless. AWS is fine. For region, we'll just stick with US East 2. And then we're just going to create our index. And yeah, you should be on the starter plan, which is just the free trial or the free tier. Hit create. And just like that, we've created the bones of our vector database inside of Pine Code. Now, we're going to have to actually connect it inside of Naden. So, I want you to go to the left where it says API keys. Click on that. We're going to create a new API key. I'll call mine part five. Hello and welcome to part six of my NN master class series where I take you somebody who has no idea what NN is or how AI agents even work and turn you into somebody who can make sophisticated AI workflows and even sell them. In part five of this series, we finally started diving into some more complex topics, namely Rag. We created our own vector database inside of Pine Cone. We set up an entire data ingestion pipeline using Google Drive. And then we connected it all together and had our AI agent answer questions about whatever documents we put inside of it. Now, in part six, we're going to continue ramping up the complexity of our AI agent ecosystem by showing you how to take whatever you build inside of NADN and put it somewhere else. Namely, in today's video, we're going to be using Lovable. So, I'm going to show you how to create your own web page using Lovable and then hook up your Naden AI agent to it. Now, why are we doing this? Why does it matter? Well, you don't want to be inside of NADN interacting with your AI agent all the time. Imagine some extremely complex AI agent you've made that has all these different tools associated with it. Do you want to have to go inside NADN every single time, open up this chat message, and talk to it that way? No, of course not. You're going to want to be able to add it to any number of external integrations, whether that's Telegram, Slack, a web page, right? Your CRM. There's a million different places you are going to want to be able to put your AI agent and have either you or your team interact with it. And to do that, you need to learn how to connect it with external stuff like lovable, which is what we're going to do today. And so, we aren't just going to learn how to create web pages using Lovable. We're going to dive into things like web hooks, how they work, what they're actually doing, and what we need to edit inside of our AI agents every single time we add some sort of external integration. And so while the endgame of today's lesson is relatively simple, which is essentially change the interface in which we interact with our AI agent, it's actually really really important to internalize this because no matter what you build, you're going to want to put it somewhere else. And the lessons you learn here are going to remain the same. How you set up the web hooks, how you test out the data, and what you need to change inside of here to get it working wherever you want. So without further ado, let's actually jump into it and head over to Leovable. I thought we were trying to create an AI workflow. So let's use this AI lead generation automation as our example for today's lesson. And this is where you also lean very heavily on AI. You're going to go to Claude Gemini chat GBPT and you're going to have it create this PRD for you. What you just need is you need success to find. I'm trying to create this right this automation and it needs features A, B, and C. And so back to our cold outreach, what was that? The PRD was what I want to create an lead generation AI automation machine, but what are the features it needs? It needs some way to scrape data. That's what you see here. Feature A. And then what's feature B? I need some way to enrich that data. That's what you see here. Feature C, I need to generate custom outreach messaging. Like what is all that? Well, it's probably the most important part of this whole thing and that's what's going on here in the red. Long story short, we're doing data ingestion, right? We're taking documentation from the business and we're putting it inside this database for AI. And so where this client had all of his stuff living was inside a Google Drive. had hundreds and hundreds of Google Docs and PDFs and he wanted it to go inside the database. And that's what's happening here, right? We have a series of triggers set up that are ready to go anytime a file gets edited or added to this folder. And then from there, we're extracting all the information from that file. We're adding some metadata information like what's the title of the document it came from? What's the actual URL of that document so we can reference it later? And then we're extracting the text and sending it to our vector database. Now, I'm not going to go super deep into what vector databases are. There's a lot of really good in-depth videos in there. All you need to know is essentially it's a highdimensional database, which means everything that's going in there is essentially being turned into a series of numbers and then it gets put somewhere on a graph where everything's kind of similar. And if that only confused you even more, that's totally okay. Alls you need to know it's a database and the AI can find stuff in there. And the one other thing you need to know is what happens to the files as it goes inside that database. So what happens is it starts chunking the documents. So what is chunking? Well, imagine I had a document that was a 100 pages long. We don't just take that 100page document and shove it into the database. Instead, we chunk it and it gets chunked based on a almost arbitrary amount of characters. Usually it's about 750 to a,000. So think every thousand words that becomes a segment and then another segment and then another segment and we throw all those segments into the database. Why do we do that? Because it just makes it easier for large language models and AI to find what it needs when it's chunked up that way. It's quicker. And so that whole process of taking information from place A, in this case Google Drive, tagging it, chunking it, shoving it into the vector database, that's all happening here in the red. And in my client's case, it was several hundred documents. You can do this with thousands and thousands of documents. And so once it's all sitting nice and pretty in our vector database, then we come up here and we can actually interact with it. In my client's case, we actually had it hooked up to chat. We're just using a chat message here just to demo, but he had a Slack chatbot. So this agent lived inside of Slack. You could just like at it and would actually respond to your questions. And the two things you'll see here is just the Superbase vector store and the chat memory. So we are able to save all the chats that go to this AI agent, they're also living in that same kind of vector database ecosystem which is superbase and we'll go into that in a little more detail later. And then we have the vector store. So anytime someone asks a question that should be able to be found inside the vector database, it's going to call on this vector store tool. And this vector store tool is what allows our AI agent to go inside the database and then try to find the knowledge it wants. Now, something that might be new to you, even if you've done rag in the past, is this re-ranker co here. Now, the reranker sub tool, I guess you want to call it, is actually rather new to Naden. It really came on the scene in the last month or so, but it's an awesome tool that makes our rag way more accurate. So, now let's talk about the reranker and how it actually works. So, I ask my AI agent a question. It then goes in the vector database and grabs a certain amount of chunks, let's say 20, that it thinks are relevant to answering my question. Once it does that, the re-ranker comes in. It's then going to take those 20 chunks and rank them in order of relevancy and it'll give it a score out of like I think it's several thousand, but basically think, all right, this first one's a 9 out of 10, the second one's an 8.5, this one's a seven. And then it's actually going to take an answer based on the best ones. And that whole process is being done by Cohhere. We're actually going to go inside cohhere soon, which is over here. It's an actual third party app. It requires an API key. So, there is a cost associated with it, but it's very, very small. Each question you ask, we're talking like 1/5 of a penny, but it makes our stuff way, way more accurate. Even if it's just slightly slower, you're not going to notice too much of a difference and slightly more expensive. Now, your first question probably is, well, doesn't shouldn't RAG be doing that already? You're telling me it doesn't rerank them? No, it doesn't. because it actually overindexes overindexes is a on is an opinion kind of overindexes on speed and cost. But with the reanker we're saying, "Hey, just slow down a minute. Now, speaking of clients, I know at the beginning of this video I said I would kind of run you through the pricing structure and how I actually created this and handed it off to the client. So, first things first, pricing. How did I come up with $5,000? Well, if you've been in the space for any amount of time, you've probably heard people talk about how they do pricing. And usually they frame it as, "All right, we're doing it value based. So, this thing is going to save my client, you know, $20,000 a month and then so I'm going to take, you know, a 30 or 30 or 40% cut of that and then everyone walks away happy." I will tell you, maybe I just don't have the best clients ever. I think I have pretty good ones, but I've never run into a client who can really accurately say how much an automation is either saving him in terms of money or how much money it's actually making him, right? Like I don't think most businesses have it on such a granular level that they are going to save this much time every single month and that translates to this many dollars. It just doesn't happen, at least in my experience. And because of that, the whole like value play for doing pricing kind of goes out the window right off the bat. So then the question becomes, well then how'd you come to $5,000? And frankly, it's kind of one part that's how much I value my time and I know how much time it's going to take me and also another part of that $5 to $10,000 is what I have found to be kind of the sweet spot when you're trying to sell these AI agents and automations to businesses. Because remember, what are you trying to sell to? You're not trying to sell to mom and pop shops that have no money. You want to be selling to like mediumish size companies that are like 1 to 10 million ARR that aren't just going to bulk immediately at something in the 5 to 10k range, right? It's not going to ruin their lives if this project absolutely fell apart. It's not going to, but they don't come into it like, "Oh my gosh, this is my last $5,000. This has to work." And so because of that, I know I can usually sell between 5 to 10. Like seven to eight is kind of the sweet spot and five for something that's kind of just like boilerplate like I've done this a bunch of times. I know I can get done quickly. Five just kind of makes sense. All that to say it's kind of vibes. It's kind of what you can get away with. Are you brand new and you just need customers? You probably have to price it a little bit more favorably for the client. Maybe 3K, maybe 4K. I personally would never suggest you go under 2 to 3K. Like you don't want to be bottom of the barrel. You don't want to do a race to the bottom because they could always go overseas and have someone build this for them for 10 bucks an hour, right? Now, we talked about LinkedIn scraping, we talked about Apollo, we talked about Appify. How do all these things actually connect? Because it can be kind of confusing. So, starting from the top, Apollo. Apollo is this program that you pay 60 bucks a month for or more if you want more stuff to get information about leads, right? The same thing that we're generating in our workflow is stuff that Apollo generates, right? names, LinkedIn URLs, emails, phone numbers, all that stuff can be found in Apollo. The thing with Apollo, like I mentioned before, it costs 60 bucks a month, and I probably don't want to pay 60 month 60 bucks a month for Apollo. So, we instead use Appify to gain the information. Now, where does LinkedIn play in all this? Well, Apollo and Apify, they're getting their information from LinkedIn. They're scraping LinkedIn themselves. So, you have these guys that are scraping information from LinkedIn. It's on their platform and we want to access it. Well, I don't want to scrape LinkedIn myself because we're worried about terms of service on our own account. So, I need to go through a third party which is Apollo. I don't want to pay the full price for Apollo. I just need to essentially like have a little slice of that Apollo pie. And that's where Apify comes in. So, Appify is a marketplace for web scrapers. You pay a certain amount a month. In this case, $40. And that essentially gives you $40 of credit to use on these different scrapers. For example, you'll see up here on the top, Apollo scraper scrape up to 50,000 leads. You pay a $120 for every,000 leads that this actor, they call it ampify actors, gives you. So 40 bucks a month for Apify. A$120 of that 40 goes to this actor every 1,000 leads it generates. So all that to say is that there's a lot of layers you have to go through to get someone's linked information due to just how LinkedIn works. And this is the way we do it. And it's not just because $40 is less than 60. It's because Appify gives us some flexibility, right? What if I don't need to use all $40 on Apollo? Well, there's tons of other web scrapers on Aify. Things like Instagram scrapers, Tik Tok scrapers, etc. Right? I can kind of just pick and choose. It's sort of a buffet and allows me to be flexible at a lower price point. Next thing I'm going to do is I'm going to give it tactical context. So, small picture. Where is my error and what does it look like? So, if my error was here, what I'm going to do is I'm going to take a screenshot of this picture, right, of the module itself. I'm then going to put that into AI and then I'm going to give it context as well. Here's what the error looks like. Here's what the error is saying. And here's when I'm seeing it. Right? So now it knows what I'm trying to do big picture. It knows what the error is small picture. And what it's going to give me back is some sort of troubleshooting steps. And if you're lucky, the first steps it's going to give you are going to work just fine. And your problem will be solved and you're on your way. If not, you're just going to repeat the process. Now, once you give it big picture context once, you don't need to continue doing that. But I wanted to show this so we can kind of talk about what Rag actually is and what we need to do to create it because we're just going to create a very simple version for today, right? Like we're all about the basics, all about the fundamentals in this video series and we need to understand how it works at a very base level before we can upgrade and start doing some varsity stuff like this. So first things first, what is RAG? Retrieval augmented generation. Now, when you talk to chat GPT, what information is it using to answer your questions? Well, it's relying on its training data, right? It's been trained on millions and millions and millions and millions, trillions of parameters to answer your questions. But what if I wanted to answer questions that are specific about me or my team or the project I'm working on? What would you do? Well, you would go ahead and you would dump some files into it, right? Like you would put in an Excel file, you would put in a PDF, you would give it documents for it to answer questions about. That is essentially a very simplistic version of rag. So rag means it's going to retrieve a document from some sort of database. It's going to augment its answer based on the documentation and then it's going to generate an answer for you. Rag. Now in this one you're looking at the database it goes to is being is living inside a superbase. And the type of database that's being used is a vector database. Now, I'm not going to go super in deep into vector databases and all the math behind it and highdimensional stuff. Just think of it as a special database that AIS and large language models love because it's easy for them to grab information from there. That's all you really need to understand. Now, the other part you need to understand is how do we get documents from wherever they are, whether that's a Google Drive or on your computer into a vector database that the large language model that our AI agent can actually use. Well, you need some sort of ingestion system. And that's what you see going on here in the red. Now the long story short of this is we start inside of a Google drive. These documents then get tagged. They get a bunch of metadata sent to them. We extract the text and then it's put into our vector database. Now one thing of note when going inside a vector database the way it works is imagine I had a 100page PDF. When it goes into the database it doesn't sit there as a 100page document, right? That would be a lot of text for it to constantly go through to find the answer. Instead what happens is it gets chunked. And you kind of can see that here at the bottom with the character text splitter. Basically, every 500, 750, a thousand characters, that document gets chunked up into little bite-sized pieces that then all go into the vector database. And because it's in small pieces, it's easier and it's quicker for the AI to be able to find the text we need to answer our question. So, it's a speed thing. So, that is something you need to know because it does have its pros and cons, right? On one hand, we get speed, but sometimes it gives us a loss of accuracy, right? You can start thinking about well how is it going to know the right answer if I shove a giant Excel file into there that's thousands of rows and hundreds of columns like if it's chunked up how does it actually have context everything great question and you are just starting to get into the deep waters that is rag and some of the intricacies with it but for today we're just going to stick with the basics which is just like Google Docs and PDFs and putting information into a database and then having AI retrieve it right once you get your hands on that then you can start trying to do some uh highle stuff with like large Excel files and putting in SQL, right? It goes it goes kind of deep. But I say all that just to let you understand what's kind of going on under the hood, right? We're going to have some documents that we want our AI to know about. We need to put them into a database. And so, we need some sort of ingestion system. Once the documents are inside the database, then we just need to add some sort of tool to call on it. And that's what you see here in the Superbase vector store, right? So, that's also a huge boon for people who decide to self-host. And why is that? Well, let's think about it. What is Rag again? It's a retrieval augmented generation AI agent which means it's an AI agent that has information about the business, about the project, about your team. So essentially it's like chat GBT has been plugged into your system because all those documents that you want AI to know about is now sitting inside a vector database which the AI agent has access to and can now answer questions about whatever is in there. And that's super valuable to any business and it's also industry agnostic, right? Everyone wants AI that can kind of act like an actual human, an actual member of their team. And Rag is a really good way to do that. And so if you're wondering, hey, what sort of AI agents do I actually need to care about and learn how to build? Rag should be number one on your list. And I think this one that we're going to go through today is a great foundation for you to kind of learn all the big parts of it. And so with that, let's actually jump into it. Starting with the AI agent, we'll do kind of a highle overview of how it works, and then I'll go through it and show you how you would set it up yourself. Okay, so we kind of talked a little bit already about the data piece, right? We're taking data from somewhere inside the business and then it's going inside like a vector database, whatever that is. Like what is all that? Well, it's probably the most important part of this whole thing. And that's what's going on here in the red. Long story short, we're doing data ingestion, right? We're taking documentation from the business and we're putting it inside this database for AI. And so where this client had all of his stuff living was inside a Google Drive. had hundreds and hundreds of Google Docs and PDFs and he wanted it to go inside the database. And that's what's happening here, right? We have a series of triggers set up that are ready to go anytime a file gets edited or added to this folder. And then from there, we're extracting all the information from that file. We're adding some metadata information like what's the title of the document it came from, what's the actual URL of that document so we can reference it later. And then we're extracting the text and sending it to our vector database. Now, I'm not going to go super deep into what vector databases are. There's a lot of really good in-depth videos in there. Alls you need to know is essentially it's a highdimensional database, which means everything that's going in there is essentially being turned into a series of numbers and then it gets put somewhere on a graph where everything's kind of similar. And if that only confused you even more, that's totally okay. All you need to know it's a database and the AI can find stuff in there. And the one other thing you need to know is what happens to the files as it goes inside that database. So what happens is it starts chunking the documents. So what is chunking? Well, imagine I had a document that was a 100 pages long. We don't just take that 100page document and shove it into the database. Instead, we chunk it. And it gets chunked based on a almost arbitrary amount of characters. Usually it's about 750 to a,000. So think every thousand words that becomes a segment and then another segment and then another segment and we throw all those segments into the database. Why do we do that? Because it just makes it easier for large language models and AI to find what it needs when it's chunked up that way. It's quicker. And so that whole process of taking information from place A, in this case Google Drive, tagging it, chunking it, shoving it into the vector database, that's all happening here in the red. And in my client's case, it was several hundred documents. You can do this with thousands and thousands of documents. And so once it's all sitting nice and pretty in our vector database, then we come up here and we can actually interact with it. In my client's case, we actually had it hooked up to chat. We're just using a chat message here just to demo, but he had a Slack chatbot. So this agent lived inside of Slack. You could just like at it and it would actually respond to your questions. And the two things you'll see here is just the superbase vector store and the chat memory. So we are able to save all the chats that go to this AI agent, they're also living in that same kind of vector database ecosystem which is superbase. And we'll go into that in a little more detail later. And then we have the vector store. So anytime someone asks a question that should be able to be found inside the vector database, it's going to call on this vector store tool. And this vector store tool is what allows our AI agent to go inside the database and then try to find the knowledge it wants. Now, something that might be new to you, even if you've done rag in the past, is this re-ranker co here. Now, the re-ranker sub tool, I guess you want to call it, is actually rather new to Naden. It really came on the scene in the last month or so, but it's an awesome tool that makes our rag way more accurate. So, now let's talk about the reranker and how it actually works. So, I ask my AI agent a question. It then goes in the vector database and grabs a certain amount of chunks, let's say 20, that it thinks are relevant to answering my question. Once it does that, the reranker comes in. It's then going to take those 20 chunks and rank them in order of relevancy. And it'll give it a score out of like I think it's several thousand, but basically think, all right, this first one's a 9 out of 10. The second one's an 8.5. This one's a seven. And then it's actually going to take an answer based on the best ones. And that whole process is being done by cohhere. We're actually going to go inside cohhere soon, which is over here. It's an actual third party app. It requires an API key. So, there is a cost associated with it, but it's very, very small. Each question you ask, we're talking like 1/5 of a penny, but it makes our stuff way, way more accurate. Even if it's just slightly slower, you're not going to notice too much of a difference and slightly more expensive. Now, your first question probably is, well, doesn't shouldn't RAG be doing that already? You're telling me it doesn't rerank them? No, it doesn't because it actually overindexes overindexes is it on is an opinion kind of overindexes on speed and cost. But with the reranker, we're saying, "Hey, just slow down a minute. I'll I'll pay a little money. I want my answers to be accurate." And that's what the re-ranker does. So that's the agent in action. That's the tooling. That's the ingestion system. === n8n Masterclass: Build and Sell AI Agents (Part 3: APIs & HTTP Requests) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6u9bBZxs25I

Hey everyone, welcome to part three of my NAND master class series where I take you from someone who has no clue what's going on in this low code AI space and turn you into somebody who can actually make AI agents speak intelligently about them and even sell them. So if this is your first time jumping into the series, I suggest checking out parts one and two because each video builds upon the other and we start at a very foundational level. So if you have no real technical background, you're not really sure what's going on inside of Naden, just start at the beginning. But if you're a little more advanced and you want to hop in right away, go ahead because today what we are going to be learning about is HTTP requests. And HTTP requests are probably the most important module that you need to wrap your head around because HTTP requests and really API calls in general are one of the most foundational tools that you really need to internalize how they work and how to apply them if you want to create robust AI agents. So let's start with diving into APIs. Now what are APIs? Well, it stands for application programming interface. And it's just a fancy way for saying this is how two programs talk to one another. So let's imagine we have two programs. Let's say we have NAN on the left and OpenAI on the right. So I'm inside NAN and I want to use OpenAI's GPT4 to do something for me. Well, how do we do that? Well, we do it with an API call. So I send a message to OpenAI and included in that message is my API key, which is essentially like a password saying, "Hey, this is who I am. This is verification of what I want that I have billing. Please do the task for me. That is an API call. OpenAI gets your request. It verifies your key and then it sends you that information back. That is an API in action. And anytime you are having a third-party program do something via an API, that's called an API call. Right? So, we're calling that's an API call. We're calling on them. That's all that means. you're trying to contact some sort of thirdparty program to do something on your behalf. And the way you send that data is via an API call. And as I said before, we did that here in OpenAI. And these things we did last time in part two where we uploaded files to a Google Drive and where we sent a draft, that was also an API call. And the nice thing is is these are actually set up very intuitively for us, right? I go inside here, I don't have to write any code. I have these boxes that I just do drop downs for, right? my resources, my operation, the email type, the subject, right? It's very, very intuitive of what information I put where. But if you were coding this, it would actually be an entire code block instead. And when I hit plus on the toolbox, you can see over here on the right, and I'll move my thing over, we have so many different third party tools we can connect to. And when we use them, those are all using API calls as well. Now, some of them, like Wikipedia, don't even require an API key. They'll just let you freely use their resources. But most of these have a corresponding API key like we saw with OpenAI. And luckily, most of them don't require such a laborious setup process with their credentials like Google did in part two. And while there is a ton of tools here, the question you should be asking yourself now is, well, what if those tool doesn't exist inside of Naden? Because you just saw, I don't know, a 100 tools, 200 tools, but there's millions of programs on the web that you probably want access to and probably want their data. How do you send an API request to them if their tool doesn't exist? Great question and the answer is an HTTP request tool. Now, backing out for a second, like we saw here, these are all like customized, right? The Google draft, right? You got the operation, the email type, the options, the actual message, right? Very, very easy to use. Each of these modules inside of Naden that's has their own special tool like Gmail, like OpenAI. Think of that as like a custom letterhead or a custom form that Google has given us. It has given N&N that says, "Hey, every time you want to talk to me, fill out this exact form." And this way, this is the Google form, right? Super easy. And to contrast that, the HTTP request is essentially like a boilerplate generic form that we are going to send to any third party that we want to make an API call to that already doesn't have a special node inside of Naden. And because of that, understanding how HTTP requests work is extremely important because in the grand scheme of things, most APIs will not be found inside of NADN, right? A lot of the really popular ones are like Google and all that stuff, but there's going to be a ton that aren't. And you need to understand how to navigate HTTP requests, and that's what we're going to do today. And so to demo HTTP requests today, we are going to be going to tavly.com. Now, full disclosure, Tavi just recently got a node inside of Naden, but I think they're a really good place to practice HTTP request for a couple reasons. One, they have really, really good documentation, so we can kind of demo what you should be looking for. They also have an API playground that I'll show you and explain what the heck that means. And lastly, Tavi is an amazing tool that we are going to use further on, so we might as well show you how to use it today. Oh, and their API is essentially free, which is also perfect. So, I want you to go to tavly.com. You're going to sign up. Again, totally free and then log in. Okay. So, here we are inside of Tavi. Now, again, Tavi is essentially like Google on AI steroids. It's my favorite tool for doing any sort of research, any sort of like basic web scraping. So, we're really going to get familiar with this tool. Now, right up front, what you see here is kind of like just an API usage, right? You can see how much you're using every single month. They give you a,000 credits for free. We'll go into a little more detail what credits actually mean later. And we also have your API key right here, which they make super easy to get to. You just click it and it's copied. Now, what do we care about? Well, anytime you go to some sort of third party website that you need to use and you need an API key and it doesn't exist inside of Naden, the first place you're going to go is the documentation, which you can find over on the left. And then you're usually going to see stuff like this quick start API references. So, often times what we want to do is we want to find the quick start or the API reference. So if we go to quick start. So first things first, understand who documentation is for. It's primarily for a technicals software developer crowd, right? So right away you're going to see stuff like pip install tavly python. You be like, I have no idea what that is. It's okay. Let me tell you what you often need to be looking for. What you're often looking for is something like this, a curl, right? And the curl is usually going to have a few things associated with it. It's usually going to have a post and then some sort of URL. And then you'll often see things like -h-h d these are headers. So -h h is a header content type application json very common. You also see this authorization right bear space tablet your API key and then the query. So right here what you see is sort of the general outline of almost every single API API request that you're going to make to again some sort of thirdparty program. One the URL. Post means I'm sending something to them, right? In this case, I'm going to send a request for them to do something on my behalf in case, in this case, research something. So, often times when you're making an API call, the first one you're going to make is a post request, right? I need to send information to them. And then oftent times if you need to make another one, it'll be a get, as in a get request. I need to get my data. I need to download something. I need to get it. If you're sending a request, it's almost always going to be post. And let me actually zoom in here so it's a little easier to see. So we have post. We have the URL. Great. We know we need that. My personal favorite is Gemini, but you can just say, "Hey, give me a system message or an AI agent with the following tools." Following tools: Wikipedia, Gmail, Send message, and Google Drive. So, it's going to be bearer capital B space API key. And don't worry, I'll delete mine after this. Then accept application JSON. Now, down here in the JSON body, and I'll bring this up. You can hardcode it to be less, more, whatever you want. Same thing with the comments and the reactions that we're getting. And so when we run this, like you saw in the demo, we have all this data here on the right. So it gives you the URLs, it gives you like some stuff about comments, the actual content, right? And so the next job is to take all this, all 10 of these posts and shove it into our AI analysis module, which is why we have this aggregate node here, right? Because we don't want the post to come in one at a time, like one post to analysis, two post to analysis. I want a big box with all the data that's called this creators post and shove it into the AI system. And so that's what this aggregate node does. Takes these 10 posts, turns it into one item that we then pass to our AI analyzer. Now, for this, I'm just using GPT4.1 mini. This isn't like a real heavy lift for it. And you'll see here in the user prompt that we're passing, right? We're passing all 10 posts, the user topic, and the creator URL. Now, for the system message, this is where we make our money, right? So, I did this. I went back and forth with Gemini to have it create this. And you see, hey, you're an expert LinkedIn ghost writer, content strategist. I go through how I want it to analyze the piece. I go through the final output, just how I want everything to work. And again, as always, this is where you need to do your editing. If you want something changed, you don't like what the output looks like, this is where you change it. And so, for the output, we have three different things, right? We have the analysis ID. So, it's the name of the influencer as well as a date and time. We have the actual playbook and then we have the actual content of the post. And to get that, we also use a structured output parser. So, we know every single time it's going to give us the output in the format we want. And we need it in that specific format because, as you'll see in the next module, when we set it all up in Google Sheets, we need specific things to go in specific cells. And so, lastly, we update Google Sheets, right? And I have a template for this sheet if you need it in my school community. But we match on the ID because that's going to be different every single time. That way we don't get any sort of like weird things where cells are being overwritten. We map the creator um here. So right like what are we doing? We're matching it from the form submission. So my LinkedIn URL is getting dragged here. The ID right dragged in there. Playbook dragged in there. Same thing with post. Same thing with topic. And that becomes LinkedIn. Um, and that becomes the LinkedIn ghost writer kind of sheet we have here, right? ID, creator, playbook, like we said, goes into hook, structure, tone, formatting, call to action, and then we have our example post, which you can easily just copy and then paste it into LinkedIn. And I know we ran through that really quickly, but that's the workflow in a nutshell, right? This is just a really simple automation, five nodes. But I also think while it might be a beginner node, it's a great one to kind of like try out because if you're like new to NN and you're just looking for something that you know can provide you value and you can use right away, I think this is a great tool. Tons of people need something to help them create quality LinkedIn posts, but also we hit on a bunch of different things, right? Like form submissions always useful. We start going into HTTP requests and we kind of get a little taste of scraping via Amplify. We do some AI analysis and data aggregation. And while this may seem simple, it's important to really internalize these things because one of the most difficult things to really master in NN is like how do we manipulate data? Like how do I really get the data I want from the left side of the equation to the right side of the equation? And often times little things like this aggregate node actually do a ton of work cuz without it this whole thing would kind of fall apart. It wouldn't really do what we wanted it to do. And then lastly, I think you can also see a great example of how do we, you know, an analyzing it is almost the easy thing, right? Like I know AI can analyze these posts, but how can I output it so it makes sense and then I can put it somewhere that, you know, a client or a user like yourself actually cares about, right? And that's where stuff like a structured output parser comes in where we give an actual actual example of like okay this is what I want the output to look like with the analysis ID the formatting the topic all that because like you saw before these things do have to match like we need a specific thing for ID for creative or playbook etc. So while simple and while we ran through that quick, we actually hit on a lot of important tenants of automation building inside of NAND. === n8n AI Agents For COMPLETE Beginners ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=un4sA2fno0A

So, one of the most confusing things when you first start getting into the AI space is trying to get a hold and understand what is an AI agent and what is it not. And many AI agent content creators like myself don't make it easy for you because you're often, you know, shocked and odd with images like this. These gigantic AI agents with a million different workflows, half of which are referred to as an AI agent half the time, even though they aren't really an AI agent, but they're an automation. But what's the difference? Why does it matter? super super confusing for anyone who's brand new. And what I wish is someone had sat me down and kind of broken it down component by component and explain to me what an AI agent is, what it does, what makes up an AI agent, and how it differs from everything else. And so in this video, that's exactly what we're going to do. I'm going to take you through what I call the five pillars of AI agents, breaking it down as simply as possible, assuming you're not technical, you're not a developer, you've never done any of this before, but you want to understand why it all works. and we'll also go into what the difference is between an AI agent and an AI automation. So, by the end of this, you'll just be only slightly confused when people bring it up. For my demonstration, I'm going to be inside of NADN. This is a no code, low code platform that allows you to build AI agents, AI workflows without actually having code and gives you nice visuals. So, it's perfect for our demonstration here today. And so, what you're looking at here is the five pillars of AI agents. is essentially an AI agent that has been deconstructed on the left hand side and then on the right hand side is essentially that same AI agent but put together. So this way as we go through it I'm going to show you what each part does kind of in a vacuum and then we'll also be able to reference it and see what it looks like as part of a whole. So first things first, what is an AI agent? Well, there's no official definition anywhere. There's no AI agent police that are going to come and arrest you if you call an automation an agent. Although some comment sections will make you think otherwise, but a great article on what is an AI agent came out at the end of last year by Enthropic. Now, Enthropic is the makers of Claude and they have a really good definition for what agents are. And so, how they define it is agents are systems where large language models dynamically direct their own processes and tool usages, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks. Now, what does that mean? That means AI agents are dynamic and that they have multiple tools at their disposal that they can use to complete a task. And so that tool usage setup is something we see over here on the right with our AI agent. Right? You see all these tools here in the bottom in the green. These are all the things that our AI agent can do. And we could have as many tools attached to that as we want. Right? Think of chat GPT but with the ability to do things on your behalf. Send emails, do internet research, right? Send notifications like Slack. anything like you think a human could do on the internet, there's probably a tool associated with it that you can hook up to an AI agent. And the agent's ability to have multiple tools at its disposal and pick the correct one for the job it's given. That's what makes an AI agent an AI agent versus an automation, which is much more linear, right? It's always going to do the same thing every time, right? Imagine if I had an automation where I drop a file inside of Google Drive, which then triggers a Slack message being sent in a group, right? That's an automation. We could even throw AI in that and have some large language model complete the message and say, "Hey, this is what got put in the uh Google Drive. Here's who should know about it." It's still it's a linear thing. It always does the same thing every single time it's triggered. Agents are dynamic. So hopefully that kind of explains what the AI agent is. And again, great article by Anthropic called Building Effective AI Agents. Highly suggest you check it out. But let's dive a little bit further into each part because I think you can break down AI agents into five main components. And those components are as follows. You have the brain of the AI agent. You have memory, tools, inputs, and outputs. Now, I probably could have done a better job color coding this so it kind of corresponds. But let's first talk about the brain over here on the left. This is this brain. This is associated with an AI model. So we see that over here deepseek, open AI, claude. When you think of chat GPT, you think of large language models. There's a model under the hood, right? ChatGpt has 4.1, 01, 03, right? That is the actual large language model. And that's what acts as the brain, right? It's what decides, hey, I'm going to use tool A, I'm going to use tool B and then gives you a response. That's the brain, right? And it's arguably the most important piece. And these brains come in different levels, right? You can have a like dumber brain that takes up less compute power like a 4.1 mini, right? or nano or we can have really really powerful ones like sonnet or opus 4 and again over here on the right you kind of see that in action you can see the tools connected with the brain now next we have memory right in this case we see simple memory and we see postgress chat memory now simple memory is essentially something that just kind of like lives on your browser almost but memory is what allows the AI agent to kind of remember your past few conversations right something that chat GPT has right it can remember your conversations sometimes a whole lot of them and most AI agents need some form of memory to actually be useful and again that can be rather simple or it can be a fullblown database like with Postgress right something that's stored everywhere remembers all of your conversation has metadata associated with it right so there's definitely levels to memory just like there's levels to a large language model but that's the second piece and on the right hand side we see that right here next we have the tools and like I talked about before right we see them here in the blue and down here on the right in the green right tools are what lets our AI agent do things, right? We see here, we see notion, we see Gmail, we see this think tool. We also see over here what's called the calendar tool and the email tool, right? And like what are these? Well, you can have your AI agent have sub AI agents, right? Your sub AAI agent could be a tool. That sub AI agent could also have another AI agent as a tool. You can have AI agents all the way down the stack, each of them acting as tools. So, just think of tools as things that they are able to do. And the nice thing when using a program like NAD, right, I'm able to just click in here and you can kind of see here on the right all these built-in tools. Now, tools are usually associated with what is known as like an API, um, an application programming interface. And an API, if you ever hear it, it's just like one program talking to another. And it's the language they use to speak to one another. So, they know, hey, I need this data when you talk to me, and I need this data back. API, two programs talking to one another. Next, we have our inputs. And that can be a number of things. That's just what you use to talk to it, right? Again, chat GBT. What is it? It's chat, right? It's just a chat interface. Or it could also be a trigger. You can have an AI agent you never actually speak to. It just does things when other things happen somewhere, right? Like we have a Gmail trigger. If I get a specific email in my inbox that shows up, I want this agent to, depending on what's in the email, do task A, B, C, or D. Respond to it, draft up a response, notify me, whatever. It could be a trigger. It can be chat. And here we actually have voice as well. So the ability to actually talk to it could be another input. That's just how you speak with your Asian, right? So we've just gone over brain, memory, tools, inputs. Lastly, outputs. What's the final product? How does it talk to you? Again, to bring up chat GPT, the final product is just a text, right? Sometimes it's code. But just like inputs can be super varied, so can outputs, right? In this case, this responds to you with a web hook, which corresponds to the voice one. So this AI agent over here on the right actually uses voice to talk to you and talk back. But again, could be a Slack message, it could be the creation of an email, it could be a PDF, it could be something inside of Excel, whatever you want. There needs to be some sort of final output though. And so those five pillars become this like we've been talking about, right? Brain, memory, tools, inputs, outputs. And the final thing I would talk about AI agents and going a little bit deeper is what is known as the system message and that's what you see here all this text. Now what is the system message and system messages are similar to prompts right? I talk to chat GBT, I give it a prompt that is called the user message, right? That's what the user is asking it to do. But every single AI system usually, well, most of them have what is called a system message. And that's essentially a set of instructions under the hood that define its logic that say, hey, this is how I want you to act. These are the tools you have available to you. This is how I want you to logically flow through something. Right? you know, when chat GBT was being like super sickopantic with people, right, not so long ago, that probably had something to do with the internal system message of it. And so system messages are very, very important because as intelligent as these systems are, they're also kind of really stupid to a certain extent, right? I like to refer to them as like genius interns. And these system messages essentially give them blinders that allow them to focus and do what you need them to do. And so that's kind of like the sixth pillar I would say. So, that was just a really quick video on this. I hope it kind of made AI agents make a little more sense to you and how they compare with automations, right? The biggest thing is they have tools and they can choose to use them depending on the task, right? They're dynamic. It's not always doing the same thing every single time and they're, you know, kind of broken up into these big parts. So, again, hope that helped you out. I know it was super confusing when I started. I wish this is how someone explained it to me. And let me know if you have any questions in the comments.
