=== Local Host Your AI Agents for Free With Docker, Ollama, and Cloudflare (n8n tutorial) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVsu45cnKrs

Hey everybody. In today's video, I'm going to be teaching you how to locally host your AI agents on NADN. We're going to go through how to set up NADN on your computer using Docker. We're going to talk about how to get AI models also running on your computer via O Lama. And then I'm going to teach you how to set up a Cloudflare tunnel so these AI systems can properly use web hooks and triggers. But the first thing we're going to do is talk about what local hosting actually is and kind of give you an overview of how all this works from a high level before we actually get in there and start building stuff. So first things first, what's the difference between cloud hosting, self-hosting, and local hosting? Cuz these terms get used a lot almost interchangeably when it comes to building NAT workflows. So first of all, let's talk about cloud hosting. Now when people talk about cloud hosting, what they're usually talking about is just using the native NAN cloud. Now, this is the native NAND cloud. It's the one you actually pay for. So, when I sign up for NAND and I give them 20, 24 bucks a month. I'm buying the rights to use their NADN cloud. Now, the positives of that are is the simplicity. Like, it's going to work. You don't have to be technical. I just give them money and I know when I sign up and I go to NAND's website, my stuff is going to be there. Now, the downside obviously is a the cost and b some of the limitations, right? I can only do five active workflows and I can only do 2500 workflow executions a month before I have to go to a different tier. And because of those limitations, that's why you often hear about self-hosting. Now, self-hosting just means you're using NADN outside of their native cloud, right? And that self-hosting umbrella also includes local hosting. But when people talk about self-hosting, by and large, they're talking about going to some sort of third party and using their cloud hosting services. So like hosting your Digital Ocean. I go to these third parties usually for cheaper and I use naden there and I'm using their community version which is a slightly different version of naden than what you get on their native cloud and using the community node actually has a lot of advantages. I'm not limited to the amount of active workflows I have or the amount of executions and I have access to more community nodes. But this video isn't about hosting on nadn or self-hosting on a third party. This is about local hosting. And what is local hosting? Well, that's just having NAND on your own PC or your own servers. Now, why would you ever want to do this? Well, there's a couple reasons. One is security, data security. If I am dealing with, you know, proprietary information or sensitive data that I don't want exposed to the web, doing some sort of local hosting probably makes sense. This information is never going to leave my server and I can make it so it never even touches the internet. And the second reason is cost. It's essentially free. I'm not going to pay nan a dollar. I'm not going to pay OpenAI a dollar. Everything is downloaded onto my computer. The only payments I have is essentially the electricity to run it and also the cost of either the server or the PC. So, those two reasons, security and price are what drives a lot of people to local hosting. And it is something you need to have in your repertoire if you're ever going to be running your own AI agency and want to sell these things because there are a lot of businesses out there that do need local hosting solutions. And so, how does it all work? Well, in this instance, we're going to just pretend everything is on your personal computer. And there's a few things that we are going to need to set up. First of all, we need to get NAN on our computer. And the way we're going to do that is through Docker. Now, what is Docker? Well, Docker is a containerization system. What the heck does that mean? Well, everyone's computer is different. Different operating systems, different updates, different software on there. And if we just downloaded Nadn straight onto it and tried to run it natively, we could have some problems just based on how compatible our computer is. And so what Docker does, and it does this for a lot of programs, is is it essentially gives us this box that NAN is going to come in where we know it's going to run properly. So regardless of our PC or what we have on it, we just use Docker to download this box that includes NAN and we run that box on our computer and we know it's always going to work. Docker is also great because it allows us to very easily do updates, pull downloads, and it just keeps everything nice and neat. And so Docker is going to be the key piece to actually getting NAND localized. Now, in this video, we're actually going to take it a step further, and we're also going to localize our large language models. And we're going to do that using O Lama. Now, what's Olama? OMA is a program from Meta and it essentially allows you to download and run open-source large language models like DeepSeek. And so what we're going to have set up here by the end of this video is you are not only going to have Nad running locally, you're also going to have the large language model running locally, which means you can use both of them to your heart's content without paying a scent. And again, that data stays on your computer. Your information isn't getting sent to OpenAI. your information isn't getting sent to wherever information gets sent on the DeepSeek, you know, cloud service, right? It's all inside this nice little box. Now, again, what are the downsides of this though? It's free and it's safe. Can't just be all great, right? Well, it's the power of the systems and the power of the system is going to be depend on the power of your server, of your PC, right? So, for example, let's talk about DeepCar1, right? Large language models are usually classified by the amount of parameters they have. the top tier cloud models, we're talking like 01, 03, gem 92.5, we're talking about billions upon billions of parameters, right? They're generalized. They have to do a ton of stuff for a ton of people. Your local models are only going to have a few billion parameters, probably somewhere between 3 billion to 8 billion depending on the power of your computer. And so what we get in terms of price and security, we do lose some of the power of the model. It's just not going to be as good. But for a lot of people, they truly aren't doing things that require the cloud top tier frontier level large language models. And something like a 3 billion or 8 billion parameter DeepSeek is going to be totally fine. And in this video, I'm going to show you how to download it, how to look at different models, how you can tell which one is right for you. And the last step is going to be Cloudflare. Now, what the heck is Cloudflare? Well, as much as I've talked about having this all localized on your computer, there are going to be times where you actually want to interact with stuff on the web. You want to be able to deal with triggers. You want to be able to deal with web hooks. And Cloudflare is going to be what allows us to do that. And if you're someone who needs external triggers and external web hooks to work for your workflows, you are going to need something like Cloudflare, right? It's going to be a tunnel and it's also going to be essentially our security guard. So when information comes in from the web that's trying to talk to RPC, Cloudflare is going to make sure, hey, it's not some sort of common malicious attack. It's going to hide our home IP address, and then it's actually going to encrypt everything, which HTTPS. So it's helping keep us safe. It's getting rid of bad actors and it also just allows us to have much more robust workflows. And so all of that is what we are going to cover today step by step. And I'm going to assume you are also not technical at all. You don't know how to use a terminal. You don't even know what the terminal is. But we are going to dive into it a little bit. And I promise you it's not too scary. I'm going to give you all the commands that you need to use and we're going to get through it just fine. And the way we are going to attack this is simple. We are going to start with Oama, right? All right, we're going to go into O Lama. You're going to be able to send information out to the web, right? Starting from you. So, I can still connect to OpenAI and talk to it and it talks back to me, right? Cuz I initiated that. But when it comes from something from outside and coming and finding our address, it just doesn't know how to do it unless you've tunnel properly. And so to confirm we've done that, we're just going to do a very simple web hook test where we set up two web hooks and we just have them talk to one another. And so you're just going to do web hook. Um we're going to go ahead and respond using respond to web hook node. And then just connect that to another web hook node. Have it respond to web hook. And we're going to do we want it to respond with text. And if it's a success, we're just going to have it say success. So how is this going to work? It's going to if the web hook properly works and we're able to access it and it talks to our NAM locally, it will then send a signal to this guy and we'll see a response that says success when we click on it. So I want you to save this, set it to active. Got it? Go inside your web hook, go to production URL, copy that, open up a new tab, and just enter it in there. Once you're there, you can refresh it, too. But you should see a success thing there. And if we go inside of here and I go to executions, we'll see the web hook worked. Awesome. If you're looking for a way to generate leads, enrich leads, and conduct cold outreach campaigns, then this is the NAND workflow for you. We then use Instantly AI to conduct a full-blown cold outreach campaign with all the information we've gathered here. Now, if you watched my previous videos, you've seen this LinkedIn scraper and cold outreach machine. And while we were essentially doing the same thing, right, which is like get linked information and do company research, this one was also missing some sort of cold outreach mechanism to actually conduct a large scale campaign. So, starting from the top, Apollo. Apollo is this program that you pay 60 bucks a month for or more if you want more stuff to get information about leads, right? The same thing that we're generating in our workflow is stuff that Apollo generates, right? names, LinkedIn URLs, emails, phone numbers, all that stuff can be found in Apollo. The thing with Apollo, like I mentioned before, it costs 60 bucks a month and I probably don't want to pay 60 month 60 bucks a month for Apollo. So, we instead use Appify to gain the information. Now, where does LinkedIn play in all this? Well, Apollo and Apify, they're getting their information from LinkedIn. They're scraping LinkedIn themselves. So, you have these guys that are scraping information from LinkedIn. It's on their platform and we want to access it. Well, I don't want to scrape LinkedIn myself because we're worried about terms of service on our own account. So, I need to go through a third party which is Apollo. I don't want to pay the full price for Apollo. I just need to essentially like have a little slice of that Apollo pie. And that's where Appify comes in. So, Appify is a marketplace for web scrapers. You pay a certain amount a month. In this case, $40. And that essentially gives you $40 of credit to use on these different scrapers. For example, you'll see up here on the top, Apollo scraper scrape up to 50,000 leads. You pay a $120 for every,000 leads that this actor, they call it ampify actors, gives you. So 40 bucks a month for Apify. A$120 of that 40 goes to this actor every 1,000 leads it generates. So all that to say is that there's a lot of layers you have to go through to get someone's LinkedIn information due to just how LinkedIn works. And this is the way we do it. And it's not just because $40 is less than 60. It's because Appify gives us some flexibility, right? What if I don't need to use all $40 on Apollo? Well, there's tons of other web scrapers on Appify. Things like Instagram scrapers, Tik Tok scrapers, etc. Right? I can kind of just pick and choose. It's sort of a buffet and allows me to be flexible at a lower price point. You might be saying at this point, that's great, Chase, but I still don't understand why there's an Apollo URL generator. What What do we care about the URL? URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vmgmva3dL44

=== How to Build RAG AI Agents: n8n Tutorial for Beginners ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nwR5519zTC8

Hey everybody. In today's video, I'm going to show you how to create your own rag powered AI agent inside of Naden. Now, I'm super excited about this lesson because I think the number one way to drive value with your AI agents is to give it some sort of rag ability. Now, what is rag? Rag stands for retrieval augmented generation. And all that means is that we're going to give our AI agent the ability to access our own personal or business documents, right? And this means we're going to take our AI agent from just like a slightly souped-up chat GPT into something that can actually act like a member of our team. By giving it access to whatever docs we want, we can now leverage the power of AI agents on something beyond just the basic training data it already comes with. This means we can feed it stuff like sales reports, meeting notes, whatever you can think of, and actually use AI to generate some insights on those that we otherwise wouldn't. And so I'm going to show you how to a build a pipeline that allows us to just dump documents into Google Drive and have it go into a vector database. I'm then going to show you how to build the AI agent infrastructure that allows us to call on that vector database to actually use those documents we gave it. And then I'm also going to explain how rag works, what a vector database is, and how this looks from a very highle overview so you can get an idea of what's actually going on under the hood. And so we're going to start here and that's going to be an explanation of what is RAG, what are vector databases and how this all works. So what is RAG? So RAG stands for retrieval augmented generation. And let's break that down. It's actually super simple. Retrieval, that means it's actually going to retrieve some information for us, right? That's going to be the information we stored inside a vector database. In this case, let's pretend it's meeting notes. So it goes in and retrieves that information. Next, augmented. It augments its original answer with the information it's now gathered from the database. And then lastly, generation. It generates a response for us. Right? Retrieval augmented generation. Go grab the information that I gave you. Augment your response based on that information and then actually generate that response for me. That's all it is. Really, really simple. But where is it getting all that information? And how do we actually give it that information in the first place? Well, let's talk about that. Now, the information is going to be residing in what we call a vector database. And that can come in a couple different forms. There's a bunch of thirdparty tools out there that allow us to use vector databases essentially for free. Two of the most popular ones you'll see inside the kind of naden space are superbase and pine cone. So what is a vector database? How does it differ from a normal database and why do we care? So let's first talk about the differences between a vector database and a normal database and why you always see people talking about the vector type in this LLM AI agent discussion. Now, vector databases can actually be pretty complicated and this could be its own video, but I think a good way to illustrate the differences is like with a simple analogy, right? Imagine you're in a library, okay? And every single document that you've pushed to your AI agent is a different book and inside this library, there are two librarians. The first librarian is the normal database and the second librarian is the vector database. So, let's say I go and I talk with the first librarian, right? standard database and I'm trying to find some information inside of Moby Dick, right? That's the document I want to find and I need information about it. Well, in order for the librarian to find that particular document and the information inside it, right? Moby Dick. I have to tell librarian number one of two things. Either the exact name of the document, right? The title of the book, or I need to tell them the author, right? I need a book by Herman Melville. And then we'll kind of break it down from there, right? I need some sort of very specific information about that document for the original type of database to actually go and find it. Okay. If I just went to that librarian was like, "Hey, uh, I'm looking for a book about a giant white whale in a crazy dude." It's going to be like, "I have no idea what you're talking about." Right? I can't find that. You got to tell me something. I need more information. On the other hand, I go to librarian number two, which is the vector database, and I said, "Hey, find me the book about the crazy dude in the white whale." It's like, "Oh, hey, I got you. That's Moby Bick. That's Moby Dig. Revan Melville, here you go. What information do you want to know about it? That in a nutshell is the difference between the two, right? Vector databases essentially the term that's often used is semantic embedding, right? It's almost taking the concept of the information and storing it in a place that it can find later versus a normal database, right? Very rigid. Here's a specific information and your query better fall somewhere within these lines. So, how does a vector database actually do that? Well, remember a vector is like an xyaxis, right? Think of like plotting stuff on a graph inside of math class, right? That's a two-dimensional vector and every point has like numbers associated, right? Same thing with a vector database, but instead of two dimension, it's thousands. And think of every point as an embedding of information, right? Again, all you need to know is that vector databases work really well with LLMs because they almost store things based on sort of concepts. So when I give it a fuzzy query of, you know, hey, crazy dude and the white whale, it's like, okay, oh yeah, I know what that's about. Let me go get that data for you. And if you understand that concept and that difference between the two, that's good enough. Now, let's dive into it just a little bit further and talk about embeddings, right? So I have some sort of document that I want to give the vector database, right? But let's imagine this document is actually a,000page behemoth. It's a giant PDF, but it's super important to my business. And I want this AI agent to be able to reference the information inside of there. Like we talked about before, when we put information into a vector database, it becomes an embedding, right? Some sort of point inside that very large multi-dimension vector. But if you have a very large, you know, document, in this case, a thousandpage PDF, is it going to store all thousand pages as a single embedding? No. What it's going to do is it's going to chunk it up. And so every 500 to a,000 tokens you decide, it's going to chunk up that document into different embeddings. So a thousandpage PDF would instead become thousands of embeddings. So what are tokens? Well, think of every single word as a token, right? An individual word is roughly a single token. And so it chunks it up for two reasons. Reason number one is context window. Now remember, every single AI has a limited context window. It's usually hundreds of thousands of tokens. Uh some are even larger, like Gemini is a million tokens, but that means it can only deal with so much information, so many tokens every single request, right? So if I gave it a PDF of millions of tokens, it just wouldn't work. It would seize up. And so by chunking it up, right, by taking this document and breaking it down to a bunch of different pieces, it can then send the individual pieces that matter back up to this AI agent, which then can, you know, retrieve the information, augment, and generate it for you, our user over here. Right? So that's reason number one is the context window. And reason number two is speed, right? If I can dive here into this database and grab the specific part of that giant PDF I need and then give it to the user, well, I can do that much faster than going through the entire document all at once every single time. So speed and context window are why it chunks it. But that comes at a cost, right? Because imagine if I had let's say a giant Excel file, right? thousands of rows, thousands of columns, and I'm asking information about like imagine with sales, I'm asking information like, hey, uh what was our highest grossing month and who was the salesperson that did it and which product was selling the most? Right? If I'm just take taking a small chunk of that document, am I really going to be getting the full context I need to answer that question? The answer is probably not. And so that's one of the limitations of vector databases and chunking in general is certain documents you do kind of need full context particularly those large Excel files right and so that's one limitation of rack well how do we fix that well there's some workarounds if you look at something right here this is one possible way to do it we have actually SQL functionality built into here and so the author of this essentially rag system is Cole Meden he has really good stuff on YouTube as Well, definitely check them out. And so I took his base, messed with it a little bit, added some, you know, functionalities and played with some system prompts. But long and short of it is what he was able to do is he added SQL functionality. What does that mean? That means he's essentially able to query the entire Excel document all at once and get around it. So that's one workaround. In reality, the other workaround is kind of just avoiding those particular documents, right? Do you always need those giant Excel documents? Are there better ways to attack that? Those are some of the limitations of rag. But big picture, hey, that's how rag works. That's how vector databases work. And then for you as the user, what does this all mean? This means you have documents that we are going to put into a Google drive which then get automatically sent to superbase or pine cone or our, you know, vector database of choice. And then we add tools to the AI agent. And you'll see that right here, right? We have this pine cone vector tool. So when I ask it questions about say meeting notes, it knows, hey, I have a vector database that has a bunch of meeting notes. Let me go take a look in it. Let me go query it and then let me bring that information back to you the user. So hopefully that kind of demystified what rag is, how vector databases work, and where you the user fall into all of it. === This AI Agent Is Your New Design Team (n8n tutorial) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HqUNnbPF67g

Hey everybody. In today's video, I'm going to show you this really cool workflow that I created inside of NADN that allows us to make our own AI design department. We combined the new GPT image generation API with Air Table to allow us to create marketing material for almost any product we want. In the last couple months, we've all been blown away by how good the new image generation is inside of Chat GPT. And this automation allows us to leverage that at scale and also include images in our API request. So we cannot just make things from prompts, but we can give the API our own image that we wanted to edit or build off of. And so I think a great use case for something like this is like building images, building advertising materials in something like Amazon, right? So for my own example, my brother and I, we're in the process of creating our own like creatine gummy brand and one of the big players in the market right now is create. And so when we've been talking about, hey, what do we want to do for our own advertising? the natural thing to do is go look at what the people who are number one in that category, go look what they're doing and do your best to like copy it or do something in that same vein. So, what you're able to do with this automation is take something like this for example, like this exact ad, go into Air Table, add it, and then add your own imagery. In this case, this is our branding. and then create an automation that sends those images alongside a prompt that says like, "Hey, I want you to take my product and replace it in this image and then have it actually spit out a bunch of different variations of that." And so what you see here is exactly that, right? It replaced the create stuff with the max branding and gives me a bunch of different variations. And I can continue to iterate on that, right? Like I could take these images, put it into Air Table as well, and give it more edits and more prompts. And I think this is a really cool automation because with Air Table, we allowed to have all this in a central location, right? I'm not just going into chat GBT, continuously dumping in images, having to scroll up and down the chat window to find what I did. No, this is all in one place. Super easy to figure out, hey, where do I put my prompt images? Where do I put my prompt? What I want the size to be, the number of images, the quality, etc. And then all the endgame results are right here in the same place for you to compare. So, I think it's super cool and it's actually really easy to use. So, we're going to go through this step by step. I'm going to show you how it works from beginning to end and then we're going to go through each of the modules in the sub workflow so you can actually build this yourself on your own. And so, what you're looking at is actually kind of a big picture overview of this workflow. Right up top here in the blue and the green, we have the orchestrator agent. So, this is where all the information from Air Table is going to be sent. And here inside the AI agent, it's going to decide to put it down one of two paths. You're either going to go down the edit image path or the create image path. Now the edit image path is if we give the API images t to work with right like if we look here at air table when I have these prompt images right these these images I was working with that would be an edit image thing I'm saying hey take these two images look at them combine them do whatever that's the edit image path and then the other path which you see right here on the right is the create image path that's just a simple prompt to image generation workflow right I just give it a prompt saying I want you to create a b or c and it's going to go through this little workflow send the prompt open API create the image and it will populate back inside of air table as well. So that's big picture how it works. Now let's actually dive into how the air table is set up and then we'll go through each of the modules one by one so you can see what's going on under the hood. But just as a quick plug for my school if you're looking for the template and other deep dives into all sorts of AI agents, I suggest you check out my community. I have a link for it down below in the description. I not only have the template for it, but I have templates for all sorts of AI agents, right? Everything from music creation to lead generation to written content. It's all here and it's all built for, you know, nontechnical users, someone who's new to the space in mind. So, go ahead and check it out. All right. So, let's begin this tutorial by going through Air Table because this is where we put all of our information and this is where all the information is being pulled from. So, it's pretty simple. From on the beginning on the lefth hand side, we just have a name, right? You can name this whatever you want. This is for your own like organization. After this, we have the action. And there's two options you can choose from, which is generate from scratch or edit/ combine photos. As you saw before, what you put here for the action, that's going to determine which route it goes down, right? Are we creating from scratch or are we combining the images? So, that's where you make that decision here. Then we have the prompt images. So, for this, all you have to do is drag and drop images into this box and it will automatically populate. Next, we have our prompt. And here, you can just write whatever you want. For this one, I just said, "Hey, you're being given two images. One with Max Gummies branding, another that's a create ad. I want you to replace the create packaging with the Max Gummies packaging." Next up, give the number of images. Right? This can it'll default to one image, but you can really do as many images as you want. Just understand for every image you create, there's a cost associated with that. If you're doing stuff at high quality, that'll be anywhere from like 15 to 20. Next, we have the size of the image. That will be 1024 x 1024. Then, you have your quality. You can choose from low, medium, or high. It's going to default to high. Then you have your status. So, the way this whole thing works is you have to have this set to ready. And then you're going to have to click the start button for the automation to begin working. And once it's done, it will then automatically change this to done. And here inside the execute tab, this is where we put in the web hook information. So if we take a look here, you will see this URL formula. And this is linked to the web hook, the production web hook. And then down here, we're telling it all the information we're going to be passing to NADN. Right? So once I click execute, once I click start, it hits the web hook and it sends all this information. And again, if this isn't set to ready, it will be kind of grayed out. You won't actually be able to start it. This is just to make sure you have all your ducks in a row before you get this image generation running and you're not just burning credits for no reason. And then lastly, we have our images created. And like I said, this will continuously update. So if I run this automation once for three images, three image will populate. And if I run it again, it will just keep adding it to here. So these don't get deleted at all, which is nice because you can go ahead and look at which ones you actually like and eventually take the one you do like, put it into another automation, another run to edit it to where it's really, really good. And so that's how the air table works in a nutshell. Again, the big things you need to do, make sure you have the correct action there. Make sure you have the status in the correct spot, and then you just hit start. So now, let's dive into each of these workflows. Now, just as a heads up, in reality, all of these are actually set up inside of their own workflows. I just have them all on the same canvas right now, so it's easier for you to see how they all connect to one another. So, to start, we have our web hooks. So, we have this get web hook. And again, this is the test URL. And when you go to production, you want to make sure you put that inside of your air table URL. For respond, we're going to respond to web hook node. And we immediately have another respond web hook node. And what we have right here in this text box. What happens here is when I come here and I hit start, you will notice a page comes up and it gets deleted. All that's telling you is that's giving you some sort of visual that hey, this automation is actually running. We got the request. Thumbs up on both sides and we're continuing to execute. And then next, what happens is that information gets pushed to this AI agent right here. And again, this one is pretty simple. Alls we're doing is we want it to take the data we push from our table and then push it down to the correct subworkflow where all the work actually gets done. And so you'll see here the only thing we're really pushing right now is just the action and the record ID. And remember the action is going to be combine or edit photos and then generate from scratch, right? And then the ID is unique to each run on Air Table. So here's the system prompt. All it's saying is, hey, here's what you can expect to be in the user prompt, right? the action and the record ID and this is your task. Pick the correct tool to use. So we have our two tools right here, the edit image tool and the create image tool. You also have the think tool here. I like the think tool on any AI agent that has multiple tools and ask actually has to make some decisions about which one to choose. It lets it like use it as like a scratch pad, right? So, believe it or not, it's already been a month since OpenAI released their brand new image generation model. We've been able to make AI images, but not with text, not on this level, not with this customizability. And I think the next step after this is being able to then create videos with this, right? Like I have another video that you've all seen where we do the faceless video stuff, you know, where we took flux images and put it into cling. But now we have like a whole like new ball game when it comes to the base image, the most important part of creating those videos. So I think this is super cool. There's a ton of cool use cases for this. And like what I kept hammering on before, like go in here and play with the prompts, right? you need to make this as specialized as you can for your particular use case. So, um yeah, let me know what you think.
